- Image analysis tools are like X-ray machines — they reveal hidden manipulation.
- Retraction databases are like watchlists — they flag repeat offenders.
- AI-content detectors are like behavioral analysts — they spot "unnatural" patterns.

> **Key Insight:** No single tool is infallible. The strength of an ethical detection framework lies in the **redundancy and cross-verification** of multiple independent tools — a concept borrowed from **engineering reliability theory**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Thresholds in Bold
Common quantitative benchmarks used across detection tools:
- **Similarity Index (SI):** Generally, a threshold of **< 15%** is considered acceptable for original work; **15–25%** warrants review; **> 25%** is flagged as potential plagiarism in most institutional policies.
- **Confidence Score:** AI detectors typically return a value between **0% and 100%**, with **> 80%** indicating high confidence of AI generation.
- **p-value threshold:** Statistical fabrication detection uses **p < 0.05** as the conventional cut-off for anomalous data patterns.
- **Z-score:** Data points with **|Z| > 3** are flagged as statistical outliers in fraud detection algorithms.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Classification of Detection Tools

The detection ecosystem can be decomposed into four operational layers:

**Layer 1 — Textual Integrity Tools**
- Operate on the principle of **string matching, n-gram overlap, and semantic embedding comparison**.
- Tools: **Turnitin, iThenticate, Urkund (now Ouriginal), Grammarly Plagiarism Checker, PlagScan**.
- Mechanism: Compare submitted text against a massive database of published works, web pages, and previously submitted student papers.
- Limitation: Poor at detecting **paraphrased plagiarism** and **translated plagiarism** (ideas copied in another language).

**Layer 2 — Visual & Image Integrity Tools**
- Operate on **pixel-level analysis, metadata inspection, and clone detection**.
- Tools: **ImageJ (FIJI), Forensic Droplets, Adobe Photoshop Forensic Tools, Error Level Analysis (ELA)**.
- Mechanism: Detect duplicated regions within an image, inconsistent lighting, splicing artifacts, and EXIF metadata tampering.
- Limitation: Cannot detect **generated images** (from GANs) reliably without AI-based classifiers.

**Layer 3 — Statistical & Data Integrity Tools**
- Operate on **Benford's Law analysis, p-value distribution checks, and replication consistency tests**.
- Tools: **Statcheck (R package), GRIM test, SPRITE, Replicate**.
- Mechanism: Flag datasets where reported statistics are mathematically inconsistent (e.g., a reported mean of 4.3 with a sample size of 10 producing an impossible standard deviation).

**Layer 4 — Authorship & AI-Origin Tools**
- Operate on **stylometric fingerprinting and perplexity scoring**.
- Tools: **GPTZero, Originality.ai, Copyleaks AI Detector, WritePrints, JStylo**.
- Mechanism: Measure **perplexity** (how "surprised" a language model is by the text) and **burstiness** (variation in sentence complexity). AI-generated text tends to have *low perplexity* and *low burstiness*.

### 2.2 Why and How — Structured Logic Breakdown

| Step | Why It Matters | How It Works |
|------|----------------|--------------|
| **1. Define the violation type** | Different breaches require different detectors | Text plagiarism ≠ image forgery ≠ data fabrication |
| **2. Select appropriate tool** | Each tool has a specialized domain | Turnitin for text, ImageJ for figures, Statcheck for stats |
| **3. Establish baseline** | Tools need a reference point for comparison | Compare against known genuine samples |
| **4. Run analysis** | Computational core of detection | String matching / pixel analysis / statistical test |
| **5. Interpret score with context** | Raw scores can mislead | A 20% similarity may be legitimate (common phrases) or fraud (copied paragraph) |
| **6. Human verification** | Tools are decision-support, not judges | A flagged paper must be read by a human reviewer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In KTU university project evaluations and research publications, the use of detection tools is now a **mandatory checkpoint** in the approval workflow per UGC and AICTE guidelines (2020 onwards). Students should know both the *name* and the *operational principle* of at least three tools.

### 2.3 KTU Reference Sheet — Detection Tools at a Glance

| Tool Name | Detection Domain | Operational Principle | Typical Output | Key Limitation |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Turnitin** | Text plagiarism | String matching + citation analysis | Similarity Index (%) | Misses paraphrasing |
| **iThenticate** | Academic text plagiarism | CrossRef + web database comparison | Similarity report | Paid; subscription-based |
| **ImageJ / FIJI** | Image manipulation | Pixel intensity + clone detection | Visual overlay of duplicates | Requires trained operator |
| **Statcheck** | Statistical reporting errors | Recomputes p-values from test statistics | Flagged inconsistencies | Only works on reported stats |
| **GRIM Test** | Mean consistency | Granularity-related inconsistency of reported means | Pass/Fail per dataset | Limited to means |
| **GPTZero** | AI-generated text | Perplexity + burstiness score | Probability % of AI origin | High false-positive rate |
| **Originality.ai** | AI + plagiarism combined | Ensemble of multiple classifiers | Combined confidence score | Aggressive flagging |
| **Retraction Watch** | Post-publication misconduct | Curated database of retracted papers | List of retracted works | Reactive, not proactive |
| **PubPeer** | Post-publication peer commentary | Open annotation platform | Public comments on papers | Crowdsourced, varies in quality |
| **ELISA Kits** | Biosafety — pathogen detection | Antibody-antigen binding reaction | Qualitative/quantitative read | Requires specific antibodies |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Similarity Index Interpretation (Turnitin-Style)

**Scenario:** A B.Tech student submits a final-year project report. The Turnitin report returns:
- Overall Similarity Index: **22%**
- Internet sources: **8%**
- Publications: **12%**
- Student papers: **2%**

**Step-by-Step Interpretation:**

**Step 1 — Examine the overall figure.**
The 22% is above the 15% caution threshold but below the 25% hard-fail threshold. It lies in the **"review warranted"** zone.

**Step 2 — Disaggregate by source type.**
- *Internet sources (8%):* Generally acceptable if matched phrases are common technical terms (e.g., "machine learning algorithm," "biosensor fabrication"). These are not necessarily plagiarism.
- *Publications (12%):* This is the most critical figure. A high publication-match percentage indicates the student may have copied sentences from journal articles without quotation marks or proper citation. **This requires direct verification.**
- *Student papers (2%):* Low, and typically indicates a prior student at the same institution submitted similar work. This is acceptable unless the current student is the *same person* reusing prior work (self-plagiarism).

**Step 3 — Apply context.**
A 12% publication match distributed across 30 pages of text (≈ 0.4% per page) is very different from 12% concentrated in a single 2-page literature review section. Concentration matters more than aggregate.

**Step 4 — Decision rule.**
$$\text{Action} = \begin{cases} \text{Accept} & \text{if } SI < 15\% \text{ and no concentrated matches} \\ \text{Review} & \text{if } 15\% \leq SI \leq 25\% \\ \text{Reject / Investigate} & \text{if } SI > 25\% \text{ OR concentrated match} > 5\% \end{cases}$$

In this scenario, since $15\% \leq 22\% \leq 25\%$, the paper enters **review status**, with particular attention to the 12% publication-match block.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: GRIM Test for Data Consistency

**Scenario:** A published paper reports: "The mean reaction time of Group A was **4.37 seconds** with a sample size of **n = 20**."

**The GRIM Test** checks whether the reported mean is *mathematically possible* given the granularity of measurement.

**Step 1 — Determine granularity.**
Reaction times are typically measured to **two decimal places** (i.e., the smallest unit is 0.01).

**Step 2 — Apply the GRIM formula.**
For a sample of size $n$ and a reported mean $\bar{x}$ with granularity $g$ (here, $g = 0.01$), the test checks whether:
$$n \cdot \bar{x} = \text{integer}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate.**
$$n \cdot \bar{x} = 20 \times 4.37 = 87.4$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**
The product is **87.4**, which is *not* an integer. For 20 observations each rounded to two decimal places, the sum must be an integer, and therefore the mean — when multiplied by n — must yield an integer. **87.4 fails this test.**

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
The reported mean is **mathematically impossible**. This is a strong red flag for either (a) computational error, or (b) data fabrication. The paper should be flagged for author inquiry.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: AI-Content Detection (Perplexity Logic)

**Scenario:** A detector analyzes a 500-word student essay. It returns:
- Overall perplexity: **18.3**
- Burstiness score: **0.07**

**Interpretation Logic:**

**Step 1 — Understand perplexity.**
Perplexity measures how "predictable" the text is. Lower perplexity = more predictable = more likely AI-generated (because LLMs optimize for predictability).

$$\text{Perplexity} = 2^{H(p)} \quad \text{where } H(p) = -\sum_{i} p(w_i) \log_2 p(w_i)$$

**Step 2 — Compare to baselines.**
- Human-written academic prose: perplexity typically **40–90**.
- AI-generated text (GPT-style): perplexity typically **10–25**.

A perplexity of **18.3** falls squarely in the AI-generated range.

**Step 3 — Examine burstiness.**
Burstiness measures the variation in perplexity *across sentences*. Human writing has *high* burstiness (mix of simple and complex sentences); AI text is *flat* (uniformly predictable).

A burstiness of **0.07** is very low, supporting the AI-origin hypothesis.

**Step 4 — Decision.**
Combined evidence: $\text{Perplexity} = 18.3$ (low) AND $\text{Burstiness} = 0.07$ (low) → **High confidence of AI generation (≈ 87%)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often mistake "low similarity index" (Turnitin) for "AI-proof" writing. Modern AI-generated text is *original* (not copied) and therefore scores **0% on Turnitin**. AI detectors and plagiarism tools measure *fundamentally different things* and must be used together.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Multi-Layer Bioethics Detection Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Research Output] --> layer1[Layer 1: Textual Integrity]
    inputA --> layer2[Layer 2: Visual Integrity]
    inputA --> layer3[Layer 3: Statistical Integrity]
    inputA --> layer4[Layer 4: Authorship and AI Origin]

    layer1 --> tool1[Turnitin]
    layer1 --> tool2[iThenticate]
    layer1 --> tool3[Grammarly Plagiarism Checker]

    layer2 --> tool4[ImageJ FIJI]
    layer2 --> tool5[Error Level Analysis]
    layer2 --> tool6[Forensic Droplets]

    layer3 --> tool7[Statcheck]
    layer3 --> tool8[GRIM Test]
    layer3 --> tool9[SPRITE]

    layer4 --> tool10[GPTZero]
    layer4 --> tool11[Originality.ai]
    layer4 --> tool12[WritePrints Stylometry]

    tool1 --> aggregator[Central Aggregator Dashboard]
    tool2 --> aggregator
    tool3 --> aggregator
    tool4 --> aggregator
    tool5 --> aggregator
    tool6 --> aggregator
    tool7 --> aggregator
    tool8 --> aggregator
    tool9 --> aggregator
    tool10 --> aggregator
    tool11 --> aggregator
    tool12 --> aggregator

    aggregator --> decision{Composite Score Evaluation}
    decision -->|Clean| accept[Accept and Archive]
    decision -->|Borderline| review[Human Review by Ethics Committee]
    decision -->|Flagged| investigate[Formal Investigation per KTU Disciplinary Policy]

    subgraph postpub[Post Publication Monitoring]
        retrwatch[Retraction Watch Database]
        pubpeer[PubPeer Annotations]
    end

    accept --> postpub
    investigate --> postpub
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flow for Similarity Index Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Receive Turnitin Report] --> check1{SI less than 15 percent?}
    check1 -->|Yes| check2{Concentrated block over 3 percent?}
    check1 -->|No| check3{SI between 15 and 25 percent?}
    check1 -->|Greater than 25 percent| flag1[Automatic Flag for Investigation]

    check2 -->|Yes| review1[Human Review Required]
    check2 -->|No| accept1[Accept Submission]

    check3 -->|Yes| check4{Publication match over 10 percent?}
    check3 -->|No| flag1

    check4 -->|Yes| flag1
    check4 -->|No| review1

    flag1 --> notify[Notify Ethics Committee]
    review1 --> verifier[Trained Reviewer Examines Source Matches]
    verifier --> verdict1{Plagiarism Confirmed?}
    verdict1 -->|Yes| penalty[Apply KTU Disciplinary Action]
    verdict1 -->|No| accept1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: GRIM Test Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reported Mean x-bar] --> B[Multiply by n]
    B --> C{Result is Integer?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Pass: Mean is Plausible]
    C -->|No| E[Fail: Red Flag for Error or Fabrication]
    E --> F[Flag Paper for Author Inquiry]
    E --> G[Cross Check with SPRITE for Granularity]
    G --> H[Final Verification by Statistician]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
Define a **plagiarism detection tool** and name two commercially available software used for the same. Mention one limitation common to both.

**Model Answer:**
A plagiarism detection tool is a software system that compares a submitted textual document against a reference database of published works, web content, and prior submissions to identify overlapping passages, uncredited ideas, or copied sentence structures. The comparison is typically performed using string-matching algorithms, n-gram analysis, or semantic embedding similarity.

Two commercially available tools are:
1. **Turnitin** — widely used in academic institutions; provides a Similarity Index percentage.
2. **iThenticate** — preferred by publishers and researchers; checks against the CrossRef database of scholarly publications.

**Common Limitation:** Both tools struggle to detect **paraphrased plagiarism** (where the student rewrites sentences in different words but retains the original structure and ideas), as their core algorithm relies on exact or near-exact text matching.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for two tools, 1 for limitation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
What is the **GRIM test**? In what type of research misconduct is it primarily used?

**Model Answer:**
The **GRIM (Granularity-Related Inconsistency of Means) test** is a statistical consistency check used to verify whether a reported mean value in a published study is mathematically possible given the reported sample size and the granularity of measurement.

It works by the principle that for $n$ observations each rounded to a certain decimal precision, the product $n \times \bar{x}$ must yield an integer (since the sum of rounded values is an integer, and dividing back by $n$ must not introduce fractions inconsistent with the rounding).

**Primary Use:** It is used to detect **data fabrication and falsification**, particularly the reporting of fabricated summary statistics (means, standard deviations) in psychology, social science, and biomedical research papers.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for principle, 1 for application, 0.5 for example field)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four-layer detection framework** used in modern research integrity systems. For each layer, name the corresponding tool, state its operational principle, and provide one example of the misconduct it detects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how the **perplexity and burstiness** metrics are used by AI-content detection tools such as GPTZero. Why is a low perplexity score alone insufficient for confirming AI-generated text? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

The four-layer detection framework structures research integrity verification into independent, complementary modules:

**Layer 1 — Textual Integrity (Plagiarism Detection):**
- *Principle:* Compares submitted text against a reference corpus using exact string matching, n-gram overlap, and citation pattern analysis.
- *Tool:* **Turnitin**
- *Misconduct Detected:* Verbatim or near-verbatim copying from journal articles, websites, or prior student submissions without citation.
- *Example:* A student copies three paragraphs from a Wikipedia article on "biosensors" into their project report. Turnitin's web-source comparison flags the matching sentences with a colored overlay.

**Layer 2 — Visual Integrity (Image Forensics):**
- *Principle:* Analyzes pixel-level patterns, detects duplicated regions, and inspects EXIF metadata for inconsistencies.
- *Tool:* **ImageJ (FIJI)** with the Bio-Formats plugin
- *Misconduct Detected:* Western blot manipulation, microscopy image splicing, or duplication of figure panels.
- *Example:* A researcher copies the same Western blot image and uses it to represent two different experimental conditions. ImageJ's "Find Maxima" and "Duplicate Finder" tools detect the identical pixel region appearing twice.

**Layer 3 — Statistical Integrity (Data Fabrication Detection):**
- *Principle:* Re-derives reported statistics from stated sample sizes and granularity constraints; checks p-value distributions against expected null-hypothesis patterns.
- *Tool:* **Statcheck** (an R package)
- *Misconduct Detected:* Inconsistent or fabricated summary statistics in published papers.
- *Example:* Statcheck reanalyzes every reported t-test, F-test, and correlation in a paper and flags any inconsistencies between the reported p-value and the test statistic.

**Layer 4 — Authorship and AI Origin:**
- *Principle:* Builds a stylometric fingerprint of the author's writing style (sentence length, vocabulary distribution, punctuation patterns) and measures perplexity and burstiness for AI detection.
- *Tool:* **GPTZero / Originality.ai**
- *Misconduct Detected:* Ghostwriting, AI-generated submissions, and authorship fraud.
- *Example:* An online reviewer notices a manuscript's perplexity is uniformly below 25 across all sections, suggesting LLM generation.

*(7 marks: 1.75 per layer)*

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Perplexity** is an information-theoretic measure of how "surprised" a probability model is when encountering a given sequence of tokens. Formally:

$$\text{Perplexity}(W) = \left( \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{P(w_i \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{i-1})} \right)^{1/N}$$

Equivalently: $\text{Perplexity} = 2^{H}$, where $H$ is the cross-entropy of the text under the reference language model.

**Burstiness** measures the *variation* in perplexity across sentences within a document. It captures how much the writing "jumps" between predictable and unpredictable phrasing.

**How GPTZero Uses Them:**
- Human writing typically has **high perplexity (40–90)** and **high burstiness (0.3–0.8)**, because humans naturally mix simple and complex sentences and introduce idiosyncratic phrasing.
- AI-generated text typically has **low perplexity (10–30)** and **low burstiness (0.05–0.20)**, because LLMs optimize for high-probability token sequences and produce uniform sentence structures.

**Example:** A 500-word essay on "CRISPR gene editing ethics" submitted by a student is analyzed:
- Perplexity = 22.1 (low → AI-like)
- Burstiness = 0.09 (low → uniform)
- The combined score flags the text as likely AI-generated with ≈ 85% confidence.

**Why Low Perplexity Alone Is Insufficient:**
Low perplexity can also occur in:
1. **Highly formulaic human writing** (e.g., legal contracts, mathematical proofs, technical specifications) — these have naturally low perplexity but are not AI-generated.
2. **Texts that have been heavily edited by humans** after AI generation, raising perplexity slightly and mimicking human style.
3. **Short, generic sentences** that lack distinctive vocabulary.

Therefore, GPTZero uses perplexity **in combination with burstiness** and other heuristics (sentence length variance, vocabulary richness) to make a robust determination. A multi-metric approach is essential because no single metric is fraud-proof.

*(7 marks: 2 for perplexity definition, 2 for burstiness, 1.5 for example, 1.5 for the insufficiency argument)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Retraction Watch** and **PubPeer** as post-publication detection tools. How do they differ from pre-publication plagiarism checkers? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A B.Tech project report has a Turnitin similarity index of **28%**, with 18% matched to a single IEEE journal paper and 7% matched to internet sources. Analyze this case step by step and recommend a course of action. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Retraction Watch** is a curated, publicly accessible database that tracks retracted scientific papers. It records the reason for retraction (data fabrication, plagiarism, ethical violation, error), the authors involved, the journal, and the date of retraction. It serves as a **reactive** detection tool — it does not prevent misconduct but ensures that the scientific community is aware of retracted work and can avoid citing it.

**PubPeer** is an open post-publication peer review platform where any registered user can post comments, questions, and criticisms about published papers. It often serves as the *first* forum where image manipulation, statistical irregularities, or data concerns are raised. Notable cases (e.g., the HeLa cell line controversy, the STAP stem cell fraud) were first flagged on PubPeer before formal investigations.

**Differences from Pre-Publication Plagiarism Checkers:**

| Feature | Pre-Publication (Turnitin, iThenticate) | Post-Publication (Retraction Watch, PubPeer) |
|---------|------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Timing | Before publication | After publication |
| Trigger | Author/institution submission | Community flagging or editorial action |
| Scope | Textual similarity only | All forms of misconduct (data, image, ethics) |
| Anonymity | Reports go to institution | Often anonymous comments on PubPeer |
| Permanence | One-time check | Continuous monitoring |
| Power | Advisory, can block submission | Cannot block; informs and warns community |

**Why Both Are Needed:** Pre-publication tools catch *obvious* misconduct before it enters the literature, but they are limited to text and cannot detect sophisticated data fabrication. Post-publication tools leverage the *collective intelligence* of the global scientific community to identify subtle issues that single reviewers miss.

*(7 marks: 2 for Retraction Watch, 2 for PubPeer, 3 for the comparison table and synthesis)*

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Examine the overall Similarity Index.**
The 28% exceeds the 25% institutional hard threshold. This automatically places the report in the **"investigation"** category, not merely "review."

**Step 2 — Disaggregate the source distribution.**
- **18% to a single IEEE journal paper:** This is the most critical finding. A high match against a *single* source indicates *concentrated* copying — typically a paraphrased or lightly modified version of one source, which is more serious than scattered matches across many sources.
- **7% to internet sources:** Moderate concern. Needs disambiguation between common technical phrases and copied substantive content.

**Step 3 — Apply the concentration rule.**
Concentrated match percentage = 18% (from one source).
Since this exceeds the 5% concentration threshold, the report is flagged for **direct source verification**.

**Step 4 — Examine the matching passages.**
A human reviewer must:
1. Identify the specific sections matching the IEEE paper.
2. Determine whether quotation marks and proper citations are present.
3. Assess whether the language is *merely similar* (coincidental technical phrasing) or *structurally identical* (clear copying).

**Step 5 — Recommended Course of Action.**

$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Allow revision with citation} & \text{if language is technical, no direct copying} \\ \text{Reject and resubmit} & \text{if paraphrased copy without citation} \\ \text{Disciplinary action} & \text{if verbatim copy or repeated offence} \end{cases}$$

In this case, given the 18% concentrated match, the report should be:
1. **Returned to the student** with a request to either paraphrase properly with citations, or replace with original analysis.
2. **Re-checked via Turnitin** after revision to confirm the new index is below 15%.
3. **Documented** in the department's academic integrity register for future reference.

If the student is a repeat offender, the matter escalates to the KTU **University Anti-Ragging and Academic Integrity Cell** for formal disciplinary action per UGC regulations.

*(7 marks: 1 for SI evaluation, 2 for disaggregation, 2 for concentration analysis, 2 for the action plan)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Detection Tools Questions:**
> 1. **Tool name spelling errors:** "Turnitin" is frequently misspelled as "TurnItIn" or "Turn it In" — both lose marks in strict valuation.
> 2. **Confusing plagiarism tools with AI detectors:** Many students state "Turnitin can detect AI content" — this is **incorrect** as of the current Turnitin configuration; AI detection requires separate tools like GPTZero or Originality.ai.
> 3. **Omitting limitations:** Naming a tool without stating its limitation loses at least 0.5–1 mark per tool.
> 4. **Forgetting quantitative thresholds:** Answers that say "high similarity is bad" without specifying the **15% / 25%** thresholds are marked incomplete.
> 5. **Ignoring the human-in-the-loop principle:** Stating that "the tool decides" is a conceptual error. Tools *flag*; humans *decide*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Detection Tools are the software, statistical, and procedural instruments used to identify ethical violations, plagiarism, data fabrication, AI misuse, and biosafety lapses in research.
- **Four Layers:** (1) Textual (Turnitin, iThenticate), (2) Visual (ImageJ, ELA), (3) Statistical (Statcheck, GRIM), (4) Authorship/AI (GPTZero, WritePrints).
- **Key Threshold:** Turnitin SI of **< 15%** = Accept; **15–25%** = Review; **> 25%** = Reject/Investigate.
- **GRIM Test Formula:** $n \times \bar{x}$ must equal an integer if mean is correctly rounded.
- **Perplexity:** Human prose ≈ 40–90; AI prose ≈ 10–30.
- **Burstiness:** Humans high; AI low.
- **Post-publication Tools:** Retraction Watch (retraction database), PubPeer (community annotation).
- **AI vs Plagiarism:** Turnitin ≠ AI detector. Use both.
- **Human-in-the-Loop:** Tools flag, humans decide — always.
- **Biosafety Tools:** ELISA kits, PCR-based pathogen detectors, biosensors (covered in lab safety modules).
- **KTU Focus:** Be able to name the tool, state its principle, give a limitation, and apply the threshold to a worked scenario.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Manipulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT522 - BIOETHICS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Image Manipulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):**
> **Image Manipulation** refers to any deliberate alteration, adjustment, or modification of a digital or analog image's content, structure, or appearance, including but not limited to changes in brightness, contrast, color balance, cropping, rotation, splicing, cloning, erasing, or the addition/removal of elements, performed after the image has been originally captured.

In the context of **Bioethics and Scientific Research Integrity**, image manipulation is broadly classified into two categories:

1. **Legitimate Image Processing** — Adjustments applied *uniformly* across the entire image to enhance visual clarity (e.g., adjusting brightness/contrast, applying linear stretches) without altering the underlying scientific data.
2. **Unethical Image Manipulation** — Selective, deceptive, or misleading alterations that distort the original scientific information, misrepresent data, or fabricate results (e.g., removing a band from a Western blot, duplicating panels, cloning background pixels to hide artifacts).

> [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEBBT522):**
> The course specifically distinguishes between *image enhancement* (ethically acceptable when disclosed) and *image fabrication/falsification* (a serious form of research misconduct).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Newspaper Photo Editor"**
> Imagine a journalist takes a photograph of a public rally showing 500 people. Two types of edits are possible:
>
> - **Honest Editing (Acceptable):** Brightening the photo, cropping out a distracting tree branch on the edge, or converting it to black-and-white for the newspaper print — the *story* of the photo remains truthful.
> - **Dishonest Editing (Unethical):** Digitally removing some people to make the crowd look smaller (or larger), duplicating a person to fill empty space, or pasting the politician's face from another event into this one — the *story* of the photo is now a lie.
>
> **In research, the photo is the data.** A manipulated scientific image is exactly like a doctored news photograph — it changes the truth that the original observation recorded.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Terms (KTU 2024)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Resolution** | The number of pixels per unit area, measured in **DPI (dots per inch)** or **PPI (pixels per inch)**. |
| **Bit Depth** | The number of bits used to represent color of each pixel (e.g., **8-bit** = 256 grayscale levels). |
| **Metadata** | Embedded textual information (EXIF data) describing the image origin, date, camera, and software used. |
| **Lossless vs. Lossy** | **Lossless** formats (PNG, TIFF) preserve every original pixel; **Lossy** formats (JPEG) discard data permanently. |
| **Forensic Hash** | A unique digital fingerprint (e.g., **SHA-256**) of the original image used to detect tampering. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categorical Breakdown of Image Manipulation Techniques

#### A. **Acceptable / Legitimate Processing**

These adjustments are applied *equally across all pixels* and do not alter the scientific meaning of the image.

- **Brightness Adjustment:** Uniform shifting of all pixel intensity values by a constant offset.
- **Contrast Adjustment:** Linear scaling of the difference between maximum and minimum pixel intensities.
- **Thresholding / Binarization:** Converting grayscale to pure black-and-white at a chosen cutoff value.
- **Color Channel Separation:** Splitting RGB channels for analytical purposes.
- **Pseudo-coloring:** Applying a *lookup table* (LUT) to grayscale images (e.g., heat maps for thermal imaging).
- **Cropping (Limited):** Removing irrelevant borders, *provided* it is disclosed in the figure caption and does not hide data.

#### B. **Unethical / Misleading Manipulation**

These actions selectively alter the image to misrepresent the underlying data and constitute **research misconduct**.

- **Cloning / Healing:** Duplicating a patch of pixels from one region to another to hide artifacts (e.g., erasing a band on a gel).
- **Splicing:** Combining portions of two or more different images to fabricate a single result.
- **Selective Adjustments:** Applying brightness/contrast *non-uniformly* across the image to hide or emphasize specific features.
- **Duplication:** Reusing the same image (or panel) in multiple figures to represent different experimental conditions.
- **Erasure / Inpainting:** Removing real data points, bands, cells, or features to support a hypothesis.
- **Resolution Manipulation:** Upscaling an image to *artificially inflate* clarity, thereby misrepresenting experimental precision.

### 2.2 Operational Workflow of Ethical Image Handling

The workflow below represents the **best-practice pipeline** that KTU 2024 expects a research student to follow.

1. **Raw Image Acquisition** — Capture the image with the original sensor (microscope, gel doc, MRI, etc.) and *immediately* save an unaltered master copy.
2. **Hash & Archive** — Compute a cryptographic hash (e.g., **MD5** or **SHA-256**) of the original file for chain-of-custody verification.
3. **Metadata Embedding** — Preserve **EXIF** data (date, instrument, settings) and any laboratory notebook reference.
4. **Working Copy Creation** — Perform all adjustments on a *duplicate* file; the raw master is never overwritten.
5. **Uniform Processing** — Apply only *global, linear* adjustments if needed.
6. **Disclosure in Caption** — Document every manipulation in the figure legend (e.g., "Contrast was linearly adjusted using ImageJ v1.53").
7. **Final Storage & Submission** — Submit the processed image *and* the raw original alongside the manuscript.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Rule:**
> If a manipulation would change the **scientific interpretation** of the image, it is unethical — regardless of how minor or "cosmetic" it appears.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Pixel Intensity Adjustment | $I_{new} = a \cdot I_{old} + b$ | Linear transform: $a$ = contrast gain, $b$ = brightness offset. |
| Histogram Normalization | $I_{norm} = \dfrac{I - I_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}}$ | Scales intensities to range $[0, 1]$; globally applied. |
| Gamma Correction | $I_{out} = I_{in}^{\gamma}$ | Non-linear; $\gamma < 1$ brightens, $\gamma > 1$ darkens. |
| Image Hash (SHA-256) | $H = \text{SHA256}(\text{file bytes})$ | Produces a fixed **256-bit** fingerprint. |
| File Size (Uncompressed) | $S = W \times H \times C \times D$ | $W$=width, $H$=height, $C$=channels, $D$=bit depth (in bytes). |
| Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Higher CR = greater loss in lossy formats. |
| Mean Squared Error (Tampering Detection) | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (I_i - \hat{I_i})^2$ | Compares original $\hat{I_i}$ to suspect $I_i$. |
| Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $PSNR = 10 \cdot \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{MAX_I^2}{MSE} \right)$ | Lower PSNR = greater deviation = more likely tampering. |

> [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Note (KTU Valuation Pattern):**
> Students often confuse *contrast adjustment* (acceptable) with *selective brightness/contrast on a region* (unethical). When asked, always clarify whether the adjustment was *global (uniform)* or *local (selective)*.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Bioethics

- **Forensic Science:** Tampered CCTV footage is detected using **PRNU (Photo-Response Non-Uniformity)** sensor noise analysis.
- **Medical Imaging:** Manipulation of MRI/CT scans can falsify diagnoses, leading to misclassification under the **IT Act 2000, Section 66E** (violation of privacy) and **Section 66D** (cheating by personation using computer resources).
- **Pharmaceutical Research:** Fabricated Western blots have led to retractions of high-impact drug-discovery papers (e.g., the **Hwang Woo-suk stem cell scandal, 2004–2005**).
- **Journalism & AI:** Generative AI image synthesis (deepfakes) is governed by the **IT Rules 2021 (India)** requiring synthetic content to be labeled.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Linear Pixel Intensity Transform

The most common image manipulation operation is the **affine intensity transform**. We derive it step by step below.

**Starting Point:** A grayscale image is a 2D matrix $I(x, y)$ where each entry is an integer in $[0, 255]$ for an 8-bit image.

**Step 1 — Define the Original Intensity Range**

For an 8-bit grayscale image:

$$I_{old}(x, y) \in [0, 255] \quad \forall \; (x, y)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Linear Transform**

The general linear (affine) transformation applied to every pixel is:

$$I_{new}(x, y) = a \cdot I_{old}(x, y) + b$$

where:
- $a$ = multiplicative gain (controls **contrast**),
- $b$ = additive offset (controls **brightness**).

**Step 3 — Enforce Output Range Constraints**

Since pixel values must remain in $[0, 255]$, we apply clipping:

$$I_{new}(x, y) = \text{clip}\left( a \cdot I_{old}(x, y) + b, \; 0, \; 255 \right)$$

The clipping function is defined as:

$$\text{clip}(v, v_{min}, v_{max}) = \max(v_{min}, \; \min(v, \; v_{max}))$$

**Step 4 — Special Case 1: Pure Brightness Adjustment ($a = 1$)**

$$I_{new}(x, y) = I_{old}(x, y) + b$$

- $b > 0$ brightens the image.
- $b < 0$ darkens the image.
- **This is ethically acceptable** as long as $b$ is applied *uniformly* to all pixels.

**Step 5 — Special Case 2: Pure Contrast Adjustment ($b = 0$)**

$$I_{new}(x, y) = a \cdot I_{old}(x, y)$$

- $a > 1$ increases contrast (stretches histogram).
- $0 < a < 1$ decreases contrast (compresses histogram).
- **This is ethically acceptable** as long as $a$ is applied *uniformly* to all pixels.

**Step 6 — Special Case 3: Histogram Normalization**

Setting $a = \dfrac{255}{I_{max} - I_{min}}$ and $b = -a \cdot I_{min}$:

$$I_{new}(x, y) = \dfrac{I_{old}(x, y) - I_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}} \times 255$$

This stretches the dynamic range to the full $[0, 255]$ interval and is the most common *acceptable* preprocessing step in scientific imaging.

### 3.2 Derivation: Forensic Detection of Cloning

One of the most common forms of image manipulation is **cloning** (copy-paste of a region). A standard detection method uses **block-matching**.

**Step 1 — Divide the Image into Overlapping Blocks**

For an image of size $W \times H$ with a block size of $B \times B$, the number of blocks is:

$$N_{blocks} = (W - B + 1) \times (H - B + 1)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Block Similarity Matrix**

For each pair of blocks $(B_i, B_j)$, compute the **Sum of Squared Differences (SSD)**:

$$SSD(B_i, B_j) = \sum_{x=0}^{B-1} \sum_{y=0}^{B-1} \left( B_i(x, y) - B_j(x, y) \right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Identify Suspicious Pairs**

If $SSD(B_i, B_j) < \tau$ for some threshold $\tau$, the two blocks are near-identical and likely cloned:

$$\text{Clone Suspect}(i, j) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } SSD(B_i, B_j) < \tau \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Lexicographic Sorting for Efficiency**

Sort all blocks by pixel values to find identical blocks in $O(N \log N)$ time instead of $O(N^2)$.

**Step 5 — Verify with Shift-Vector Analysis**

Genuine identical regions (e.g., tiled patterns) form shift-vectors on a regular grid. Cloned regions form **isolated, irregular shift-vectors**, confirming manipulation.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Acceptable vs. Unacceptable

| Manipulation Type | Applied Region | Disclosed? | Verdict (KTU Ethics) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Global brightness $\pm b$ | Entire image | Yes | ✅ Acceptable |
| Global contrast scaling | Entire image | Yes | ✅ Acceptable |
| Histogram equalization | Entire image | Yes | ✅ Acceptable |
| Gamma correction (global) | Entire image | Yes | ✅ Acceptable |
| Limited cropping (irrelevant borders) | Edges only | Yes | ✅ Acceptable |
| Pseudo-coloring with LUT | Entire image | Yes | ✅ Acceptable |
| Selective brightness on a sub-region | Partial | No | ❌ Unethical |
| Cloning to hide a band | Partial | No | ❌ Unethical (Fabrication) |
| Splicing two different images | Full | No | ❌ Unethical (Falsification) |
| Duplicating a panel as a "new" experiment | Full | No | ❌ Unethical (Plagiarism of data) |
| Upscaling a low-res image to "improve" clarity | Full | No | ❌ Unethical (Misrepresentation) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Ethical Image Handling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Raw Image Captured by Sensor]) --> A1[Compute SHA-256 Hash]
    A1 --> A2[Embed EXIF Metadata]
    A2 --> A3[Store as Read-Only Master File]
    A3 --> B1[Create Working Duplicate]
    B1 --> B2{Manipulation Needed?}
    B2 -- No --> C1[Submit Original]
    B2 -- Yes --> C2{Global and Linear?}
    C2 -- No --> D1[STOP: Unethical Manipulation Detected]
    C2 -- Yes --> C3[Apply Adjustment]
    C3 --> C4[Record in Lab Notebook]
    C4 --> C5{Result Changes Scientific Interpretation?}
    C5 -- Yes --> D2[STOP: Reject and Revert]
    C5 -- No --> C6[Disclose in Figure Caption]
    C6 --> C7[Submit Processed Image with Raw Original]
    D1 --> E1[Research Misconduct: Report to IEC IRB]
    D2 --> E1
    C1 --> F1([Compliance Achieved])
    C7 --> F1
    E1 --> F2([Disciplinary Action Initiated])

    style A0 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style E1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style F2 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#660000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Image Manipulation Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROOT([Image Manipulation]) --> P1[Acceptable Processing]
    ROOT --> P2[Unethical Manipulation]
    P1 --> Q1[Global Brightness]
    P1 --> Q2[Global Contrast]
    P1 --> Q3[Histogram Normalization]
    P1 --> Q4[Gamma Correction]
    P1 --> Q5[Thresholding]
    P1 --> Q6[Limited Cropping with Disclosure]
    P2 --> R1[Cloning and Healing]
    P2 --> R2[Selective Regional Adjustment]
    P2 --> R3[Splicing and Compositing]
    P2 --> R4[Panel Duplication]
    P2 --> R5[Erasure of Data Points]
    P2 --> R6[Resolution Inflation]
    P2 --> R7[Synthetic Generation using AI]

    style ROOT fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Institutional Response to Detected Manipulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0([Manipulation Suspected or Detected]) --> S1[Initial Screening by Editor or Reviewer]
    S1 --> S2{Forensic Tools Confirm Tampering?}
    S2 -- No --> S3[Close Inquiry: No Misconduct]
    S2 -- Yes --> S4[Notify Corresponding Author]
    S4 --> S5[Author Response Window: 14 Days]
    S5 --> S6{Author Admits or Provides Raw Data?}
    S6 -- Raw Data Clears Concern --> S7[Issue Correction Not Retraction]
    S6 -- Admits Misconduct --> S8[Retract Publication]
    S6 -- No Response or Denial --> S9[Independent Investigation by IEC]
    S9 --> S10[Refer to UGC or Funding Agency]
    S8 --> S11[Notify Author Institution]
    S7 --> S12([Resolution Documented])
    S8 --> S12
    S3 --> S12
    S9 --> S13[Possible Sanctions: 5 to 10 Year Funding Ban]
    S10 --> S13
    S11 --> S13

    style S0 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style S7 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style S8 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style S13 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#660000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Define image manipulation. Differentiate between acceptable image processing and unethical image manipulation in scientific publications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Image manipulation is the deliberate alteration of a digital image after its original capture, including changes to brightness, contrast, color, structure, or content.

**Acceptable Processing (1 Mark):** Adjustments that are applied *uniformly* across the entire image (global brightness, global contrast, histogram normalization, gamma correction) and do not alter the underlying scientific data, provided they are disclosed in the figure caption.

**Unethical Manipulation (1 Mark):** Selective, localized, or hidden alterations such as cloning, splicing, erasing data, duplicating panels, or non-uniform adjustments that distort the original scientific information and constitute research misconduct (fabrication/falsification).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**List any three common techniques of unethical image manipulation and explain why each is considered misconduct.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Cloning (1 Mark):** Copying a patch of pixels from one area and pasting it over another to hide artifacts (e.g., erasing a band on a Western blot). It is misconduct because it misrepresents experimental results.
2. **Splicing (1 Mark):** Combining portions of two or more different images to create a single fabricated result. It is misconduct because it presents non-existent data as real.
3. **Selective Regional Adjustment (1 Mark):** Applying brightness/contrast to only a sub-region of the image to hide or emphasize features. It is misconduct because the data is no longer reproducible from the original capture.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a) Classify the various types of image manipulation techniques used in scientific research. For each category, give two examples and state whether the practice is ethically acceptable. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Category 1: Acceptable Image Processing (Uniform/Global Adjustments) (2 Marks)**

- **Brightness adjustment:** Global shift $I_{new} = I_{old} + b$ applied equally to all pixels — **Acceptable** when disclosed.
- **Contrast adjustment:** Global scaling $I_{new} = a \cdot I_{old}$ applied equally to all pixels — **Acceptable** when disclosed.

**Category 2: Acceptable Image Processing (Structural, with Disclosure) (1 Mark)**

- **Thresholding/Binarization:** Converting grayscale to binary at a chosen cutoff — **Acceptable** when disclosed.
- **Limited cropping:** Removing irrelevant borders only — **Acceptable** when disclosed in caption.

**Category 3: Unethical Image Manipulation (Selective/Deceptive) (2 Marks)**

- **Cloning:** Copying a region to hide an artifact — **Unethical**, constitutes *falsification*.
- **Splicing:** Combining different images — **Unethical**, constitutes *fabrication*.

**Category 4: Unethical Image Manipulation (Misrepresentation) (1 Mark)**

- **Panel duplication:** Reusing the same image in multiple figures as different experiments — **Unethical**, constitutes *plagiarism of data*.
- **Resolution inflation:** Upscaling a low-resolution image to imply higher precision — **Unethical**, constitutes *misrepresentation*.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The line between acceptable and unacceptable manipulation is drawn by **three tests**: (i) Is the adjustment *uniform* or *selective*? (ii) Does it change the *scientific interpretation*? (iii) Is it *disclosed* in the figure caption? If any answer is unfavorable, the practice is unethical.

---

**(b) Explain the workflow of ethical image handling in research, from raw capture to publication. Mention at least four checkpoints where misconduct can be detected or prevented. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Stage 1 — Raw Image Acquisition (1 Mark):** The image is captured by the sensor (microscope, gel doc system, MRI, etc.) and saved immediately. A *read-only master copy* is archived, and a *working duplicate* is created for any subsequent processing.

**Stage 2 — Hashing and Metadata Embedding (1 Mark):** A cryptographic hash (e.g., **SHA-256**) is computed and stored alongside the original. **EXIF metadata** (date, instrument, settings) is preserved to establish chain of custody.

**Stage 3 — Adjustment on Working Copy (2 Marks):** Only *global, linear* adjustments are permitted. The linear transform $I_{new} = a \cdot I_{old} + b$ is applied uniformly. The *raw master* is never modified.

**Stage 4 — Disclosure in Caption (1 Mark):** Every adjustment is documented in the figure legend (e.g., "Contrast was linearly enhanced using ImageJ v1.53; raw image archived as Supplementary File S1").

**Stage 5 — Submission and Peer Review (2 Marks):** Both the processed image and the raw original are submitted. Reviewers and journals may use forensic tools (e.g., **ImageJ**, **Forensic JPEG Folding**, **FotoForensics**) to detect anomalies.

**Four Detection/Prevention Checkpoints (Additional 1 Mark distributed):**

1. **At Capture:** Hash & archive raw image.
2. **At Processing:** Ensure adjustments are uniform and disclosed.
3. **At Submission:** Submit raw + processed together.
4. **At Peer Review:** Forensic tools re-analyze the image for splicing, cloning, or duplication.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a) What are the ethical and legal consequences of image manipulation in research? Discuss with reference to at least two real-world cases. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Ethical Consequences (3 Marks):**

1. **Erosion of Public Trust:** Manipulated images in published research mislead the scientific community, the funding agencies, and the public, undermining the credibility of the entire field.
2. **Misallocation of Resources:** Follow-up studies, clinical trials, and grants based on fabricated data waste taxpayer money and delay genuine scientific progress.
3. **Harm to Patients:** In biomedical research, manipulated clinical images (e.g., drug-trial data) can lead to unsafe or ineffective treatments reaching patients.

**Legal Consequences (1 Mark):** Under the **Indian IT Act 2000**, image forgery in official records can attract criminal prosecution. Funding agencies (e.g., **DBT, DST, UGC**) impose **5–10 year funding bans** on researchers found guilty of misconduct. Journals issue **retractions**, and academic degrees may be revoked.

**Real-World Case 1 — Hwang Woo-suk Stem Cell Scandal (2004–2005) (1.5 Marks):** The South Korean researcher fabricated images of patient-specific stem cell lines published in *Science*. The manipulation was detected by careful forensic analysis of the figures. The papers were retracted, his national funding was withdrawn, and he was convicted of embezzlement and bioethics law violations.

**Real-World Case 2 — RIKEN STAP Cell Scandal (2014) (1.5 Marks):** Japanese researcher Haruko Obokata published what were claimed to be "stimulus-triggered acquisition of pluripotency" cells, with manipulated images in *Nature*. Independent forensic analysis revealed image duplication and splicing. The paper was retracted, Obokata resigned, and RIKEN implemented stricter image-handling protocols.

---

**(b) Describe the technical methods used in image forensics to detect manipulation. Explain at least three methods with their underlying mathematical principles. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Method 1 — Clone / Copy-Move Detection (2 Marks):**

- **Principle:** A cloned region is a near-perfect duplicate of another region in the same image.
- **Procedure:** The image is divided into overlapping $B \times B$ blocks. For each pair of blocks $(B_i, B_j)$, the **Sum of Squared Differences (SSD)** is computed:

$$SSD(B_i, B_j) = \sum_{x=0}^{B-1} \sum_{y=0}^{B-1} \left( B_i(x, y) - B_j(x, y) \right)^2$$

- **Detection:** Pairs with $SSD < \tau$ (a small threshold) are flagged as suspected clones. Genuine repeated patterns (tiles) form regular shift-vectors; cloned regions form irregular ones.

**Method 2 — JPEG Compression Inconsistency (2 Marks):**

- **Principle:** A spliced image is often re-saved at a different JPEG quality than the original.
- **Procedure:** The image is divided into $8 \times 8$ pixel blocks (the JPEG DCT block size). For each block, the **quantization table** is estimated. Blocks with inconsistent quantization signatures reveal tampering.

$$Q_{est}(u, v) = \text{round}\left( \dfrac{F(u, v)}{F_{recompressed}(u, v)} \right)$$

where $F$ is the DCT coefficient matrix.

**Method 3 — Photo-Response Non-Uniformity (PRNU) Analysis (2 Marks):**

- **Principle:** Every camera sensor has a unique noise fingerprint due to manufacturing imperfections. This fingerprint is consistent across all images taken by the same camera.
- **Procedure:** The PRNU pattern $K$ is extracted from a reference image and correlated with the suspect image:

$$\rho = \dfrac{\sum (I - \bar{I})(K - \bar{K})}{\sqrt{\sum (I - \bar{I})^2 \sum (K - \bar{K})^2}}$$

A high correlation $\rho$ confirms the image is from the same camera; a low $\rho$ in a region indicates the region was sourced from a different camera (i.e., spliced).

**Method 4 (Bonus) — Metadata and EXIF Analysis (1 Mark):** Inconsistencies in the EXIF metadata (date, software, camera model) relative to the visual content can reveal manipulation. Forensic tools like **ExifTool** and **FotoForensics** automate this check.

---

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "image processing" with "image manipulation."** Image processing is the broad, neutral term; image manipulation specifically refers to *intentional* alteration. The KTU 2024 paper tests this distinction.
> 2. **Always mention "disclosure"** as a key factor. A globally adjusted image *without* disclosure is also considered unethical.
> 3. **For numerical questions, write the formula first, then substitute the values** — not the other way around. KTU examiners award partial marks for the correct formula even if the arithmetic is wrong.
> 4. **Do not state a manipulation is "minor" or "cosmetic"** as a defense. The KTU rubric judges manipulation by its *effect on scientific interpretation*, not its visual size.
> 5. **For real-world case questions**, always cite the *year* and the *journal* if known. Examiners award bonus marks for specific, dated examples.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Review)**

- [x] **Image Manipulation** = any intentional post-capture alteration of an image.
- [x] The **three ethical tests** for any manipulation: *Is it uniform? Does it change interpretation? Is it disclosed?*
- [x] **Global adjustments** (brightness, contrast, gamma) on the *entire* image are **acceptable** with disclosure.
- [x] **Selective/regional** adjustments are **always unethical** and constitute falsification.
- [x] **Cloning, splicing, panel duplication, data erasure** are all forms of research misconduct.
- [x] The **linear pixel transform** is $I_{new} = a \cdot I_{old} + b$ with clipping to $[0, 255]$.
- [x] **Histogram normalization** stretches the dynamic range to $[0, 255]$ and is the most common acceptable preprocessing.
- [x] **SHA-256** hashing of the original image is a best-practice chain-of-custody step.
- [x] **Forensic detection methods:** clone detection (SSD-based), JPEG quantization analysis, PRNU sensor noise, EXIF metadata check.
- [x] **Real-world cases** to remember: *Hwang Woo-suk (2004–2005, Science)*, *Obokata STAP cells (2014, Nature)*.
- [x] **Legal framework in India:** IT Act 2000 (Sections 66E, 66D), UGC/DST/DBT funding bans, IT Rules 2021 for synthetic content.
- [x] Always archive the **raw original** alongside the processed image — never overwrite the master.
- [x] **Disclose every adjustment** in the figure caption: tool, version, parameters used.
- [x] **PSNR** and **MSE** are quantitative metrics used to detect image deviation.
- [x] **Lossless formats** (TIFF, PNG) are preferred in research; **lossy** (JPEG) is discouraged for primary data.
- [x] The KTU 2024 course outcome tested: *CO1 — Understanding ethical principles in biological research*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethical Violations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT522 - BIOETHICS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ethical Violations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Bioethics and Biosafety (PEBBT522)**, an **Ethical Violation** is formally defined as any deliberate, negligent, or unintentional act that transgresses the established moral principles, professional codes of conduct, regulatory statutes, or institutional policies governing life-sciences research, clinical practice, biotechnology deployment, and environmental stewardship. These violations undermine the foundational pillars of bioethics: **Autonomy**, **Beneficence**, **Non-maleficence**, and **Justice** (collectively the Beauchamp & Childress principles).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> An *ethical violation* in biotechnology and biomedical sciences refers to a breach of codified ethical norms, institutional review board (IRB) protocols, or international declarations (e.g., the Nuremberg Code, the Declaration of Helsinki, the Belmont Report) that govern the conduct of research on human subjects, animals, genetic material, and ecosystems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Rules of Science" Intuition

Imagine science and biotechnology as a busy multi-lane highway. **Ethics** are the lane markings, traffic signals, and speed limits that keep the system functioning safely.

- An **ethical violation** is analogous to a driver **jumping a red light** (deliberate harm), **driving drunk** (negligent misconduct), or **driving without a license** (operating without informed consent/approval).
- Just as traffic violations can cause **accidents, casualties, and legal prosecution**, ethical violations in biotechnology can cause **patient harm, ecological damage, scientific fraud, and institutional collapse**.

The unifying intuition: **Violations convert scientific "freedom" into scientific "anarchy," eroding public trust and risking human/animal welfare.**

### 1.3 Key Terminology — High-Yield Glossary

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **Misconduct** | Fabrication, falsification, or plagiarism (FFP) in research |
| **Maleficence** | Causing deliberate or avoidable harm |
| **Informed Consent** | Voluntary, informed agreement by a participant |
| **IRB / IEC** | Institutional Review Board / Independent Ethics Committee |
| **Whistleblower** | Person exposing unethical practices internally or externally |
| **Vulnerability** | A participant's diminished capacity to protect their own interests |

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Frequency KTU Term:** The phrase *"breach of fiduciary duty"* is a frequent board-exam answer-anchor. It refers to the betrayal of trust between researcher/clinician and the subject/patient.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomic Decomposition of Ethical Violations

Ethical violations in biotechnology are best understood as a **four-tier hierarchical taxonomy**. Each tier is a *distinct logical class* of breach, not a severity grade.

**Tier 1 — Research Misconduct Violations (Scientific Integrity Domain)**
- **Fabrication**: Inventing data that does not exist.
- **Falsification**: Manipulating research materials, equipment, or processes, or altering/omitting data such that research is not accurately represented.
- **Plagiarism**: Appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results, or words without giving appropriate credit.

**Tier 2 — Clinical and Biomedical Practice Violations (Patient Welfare Domain)**
- Performing procedures without informed consent.
- Breach of confidentiality (HIPAA-style data leakage).
- Therapeutic misconception — misleading patients about experimental procedures.
- Withholding standard care to enroll in trials.

**Tier 3 — Environmental and Biosafety Violations (Ecological Domain)**
- Unauthorized release of Genetically Modified Organisms (GMOs).
- Improper disposal of biohazardous waste.
- Bypassing Biosafety Level (BSL) protocols (e.g., BSL-1, BSL-2, BSL-3, BSL-4 containment).
- Dual-use research of concern (DURC) — research with benign intent but malicious potential.

**Tier 4 — Governance and Publication Violations (Institutional Domain)**
- Authorship disputes (ghost/guest authorship).
- Conflict of interest concealment.
- Salami slicing of data into multiple publications.
- Failure to report adverse events.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Causal Logic of Violations

The **Why** behind violations is rarely pure malice. Research ethics literature identifies three pressure vectors:

1. **Publish-or-Perish Pressure** → career advancement incentives.
2. **Commercialization Pressure** → patent and funding conflicts.
3. **Cognitive Bias** → self-deception, confirmation bias, sunk-cost fallacy.

The **How** is the mechanism of detection or concealment — typically through *forensic data analysis*, *statistical anomaly detection*, or *whistleblower disclosure*.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield "Formula" Sheet — Conceptual Decision Matrix

> Since bioethics is a non-quantitative discipline, the "formula sheet" is replaced by a **Conceptual Decision Matrix** mapping violation type to consequence, authority, and remedy.

| Violation Class | Core Principle Breached | Reporting Authority | Typical Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fabrication / Falsification | Honesty, Objectivity | IRB / Research Integrity Office | Retraction, Job loss, Criminal prosecution |
| Plagiarism | Intellectual Property | University Ethics Committee | Retraction, Sanctions, Copyright suit |
| Informed Consent Breach | Autonomy | IEC / Hospital Ethics Board | Civil liability, License revocation |
| Patient Harm | Non-maleficence | Medical Council of India / State Board | License suspension, Criminal trial |
| Unauthorized GMO Release | Justice / Precaution | GEAC (Genetic Engineering Appraisal Committee) | Fines, Environmental remediation |
| DURC Misuse | Beneficence / Public Safety | National Biosafety Authority | Criminal prosecution under IT/WMD Acts |
| Authorship Fraud | Accountability | COPE (Committee on Publication Ethics) | Retraction, Blacklisting |
| BSL Protocol Bypass | Non-maleficence | Institutional Biosafety Committee (IBSC) | Facility shutdown, Funding withdrawal |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Biomedical Utility

Understanding ethical violations is not merely academic — it directly informs:
- **Clinical Trial Design** (ICH-GCP compliance)
- **Medical Device Approval** (CDSCO / US-FDA pathways)
- **Synthetic Biology Project Planning** (gene drive and gain-of-function reviews)
- **Bioprocess Engineering** (contained bioreactor design respecting BSL-3/BSL-4 standards)
- **AI-in-Healthcare deployment** (algorithmic bias and patient consent)

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Strategy:** Always explicitly *name the violated principle* (e.g., "The Tuskegee study violated the principle of Justice because researchers withheld treatment from a vulnerable African-American population"). Examiners reward principle-mapping heavily.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Case-Based Violation Detection

> [!NOTE]
> In bioethics, "derivations" are replaced by **structured analytical arguments** that walk from facts → identification → principle → consequence. Below is an exhaustive step-by-step framework.

### 3.1 The KTU Six-Step Violation Analysis Algorithm

**Step 1 — Establish the Factual Record**
Collect the **who, what, when, where, why** of the action under scrutiny. Without verified facts, no ethical verdict is possible.

**Step 2 — Identify the Stakeholders**
Map primary subjects (patient, animal, ecosystem, public), secondary actors (researcher, sponsor, institution), and tertiary observers (regulators, public).

**Step 3 — Classify the Violation Tier**
Assign the case to Tier 1, 2, 3, or 4 from the taxonomy above.

**Step 4 — Map to the Four Principles**
$$
\text{Violation} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \{\text{Autonomy},\; \text{Beneficence},\; \text{Non-maleficence},\; \text{Justice}\}
$$

At least one principle is breached; often multiple are breached simultaneously.

**Step 5 — Determine Regulatory Jurisdiction**
Identify whether the case falls under:
- Institutional (IRB / IBSC)
- National (ICMR, GEAC, DBT, CDSCO)
- International (WHO, ICH, OECD)

**Step 6 — Recommend Remedy & Prevention**
Recommend corrective (retraction, apology, retraining), punitive (sanctions, prosecution), and systemic (policy reform) actions.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Case — A 14-Mark Style Board Question

**Case Scenario:** *Dr. X, a biotechnology researcher, submits a paper claiming to have successfully edited the CCR5 gene in 12 human embryos. Upon investigation, only 3 embryos were actually edited; the remaining 9 were reported as successfully edited without supporting raw data. Furthermore, the consent forms signed by the donors did not mention germline editing.*

**Step 1 — Factual Record**
- 12 embryos claimed, only 3 actually edited.
- 9 entries appear to be **fabricated**.
- Donors were not informed about germline editing nature of the study.
- Both scientific and consent violations are present.

**Step 2 — Stakeholders**
- Primary: Embryos and their donor couples.
- Secondary: Dr. X, the institution, the funding body, scientific community.
- Tertiary: Public trust in gene-editing research.

**Step 3 — Violation Classification**
- **Tier 1 (Fabrication)**: Reporting non-existent experimental results.
- **Tier 2 (Informed Consent Breach)**: Failure to disclose germline editing, which has irreversible heritable consequences.

**Step 4 — Principle Mapping**
$$
\text{Fabrication} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Broken: Honesty, Non-maleficence (misleads future research)}
$$
$$
\text{Consent Breach} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Broken: Autonomy, Justice}
$$

**Step 5 — Regulatory Jurisdiction**
- Institutional: IRB + Research Integrity Office.
- National: ICMR (Indian Council of Medical Research) and DBT (Department of Biotechnology).
- International: Given germline editing, potentially the **WHO Committee on Human Genome Editing**.

**Step 6 — Remedy**
- **Corrective:** Formal retraction in the journal, public corrigendum.
- **Punitive:** Institutional inquiry, funding agency debarment, possible criminal liability under biomedical research statutes.
- **Systemic:** Mandatory IRB training, third-party raw-data audit before publication, standardized consent templates for heritable research.

### 3.3 Decision Flowchart Logic (Inline Textual Representation)

$$
\text{Observed Action} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Does data exist?} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Yes?} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Continue}
$$
$$
\text{Observed Action} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Consent obtained?} \;\rightarrow\; \text{No?} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Autonomy Violation Flagged}
$$
$$
\text{Consent?} \rightarrow \text{Full disclosure?} \rightarrow \text{No?} \rightarrow \text{Autonomy + Beneficence Violation}
$$
$$
\text{Containment followed?} \rightarrow \text{No?} \rightarrow \text{Non-maleficence + Environmental Justice Violation}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Ethical Violations

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Ethical Violation Event Detected] --> nodeB{Violation Domain}
    nodeB -->|Scientific Integrity| nodeC[Research Misconduct]
    nodeB -->|Patient Welfare| nodeD[Clinical Practice Breach]
    nodeB -->|Ecology and Biosafety| nodeE[Environmental Violation]
    nodeB -->|Institutional Governance| nodeF[Publication or Authorship Violation]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Fabrication]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Falsification]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Plagiarism]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[Informed Consent Breach]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Confidentiality Breach]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Therapeutic Misconception]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[Unauthorized GMO Release]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[BSL Protocol Bypass]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Dual Use Research Concern]

    nodeF --> nodeF1[Ghost or Guest Authorship]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Conflict of Interest Concealment]
    nodeF --> nodeF3[Salami Publication]

    nodeC1 --> nodeX[Reporting: IRB or Research Integrity Office]
    nodeD1 --> nodeY[Reporting: IEC or Hospital Ethics Board]
    nodeE1 --> nodeZ[Reporting: GEAC or National Biosafety Authority]
    nodeF1 --> nodeW[Reporting: COPE or University Ethics Committee]

    nodeX --> nodeOut[Remedy: Retraction, Sanction, Prosecution]
    nodeY --> nodeOut
    nodeZ --> nodeOut
    nodeW --> nodeOut
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Stakeholder Impact Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    actor1[Researcher or Clinician] -->|Action| event[Ethical Violation Event]
    actor2[Sponsor or Funding Agency] -->|Pressure| event
    actor3[Regulator or IRB] -->|Oversight Gap| event

    event -->|Harm| target1[Human Subject]
    event -->|Harm| target2[Animal Subject]
    event -->|Harm| target3[Ecosystem]
    event -->|Harm| target4[Public Trust in Science]
    event -->|Harm| target5[Scientific Record Integrity]

    target1 --> remedy[Remedy: Retribution, Compensation, Policy Reform]
    target2 --> remedy
    target3 --> remedy
    target4 --> remedy
    target5 --> remedy
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Four-Principle Breach Mapping

```mermaid
graph TB
    p1[Autonomy] -->|Informed Consent| viol1[Violation 1: No Disclosure]
    p2[Beneficence] -->|Do Good| viol2[Violation 2: Withholding Standard Care]
    p3[Non-maleficence] -->|Do No Harm| viol3[Violation 3: Fabrication Leading to False Clinical Use]
    p4[Justice] -->|Fair Distribution| viol4[Violation 4: Selective Population Exploitation]

    viol1 -.-> case[Concrete Case Mapping]
    viol2 -.-> case
    viol3 -.-> case
    viol4 -.-> case
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]**
> **Q.** Define the term *"ethical violation"* in the context of biotechnology research. Give **two** examples.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An ethical violation in biotechnology is a breach of the codified moral, legal, or professional standards governing life-sciences research, clinical practice, and biosafety. Such violations compromise the integrity of the scientific record, the welfare of research subjects, and the safety of the environment. *Example 1:* Plagiarism in a research paper, where another scientist's results are presented as one's own. *Example 2:* Conducting a clinical trial on a vulnerable population (e.g., impoverished patients) without obtaining true informed consent, thereby violating the principles of Autonomy and Justice.
*(Word count: ~70 words; mark distribution: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]**
> **Q.** What is *"research misconduct"*? List its **three** recognized categories.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Research misconduct refers to the violation of professional ethics in the conduct of scientific research. It is universally classified into three categories: **(i) Fabrication** — the invention of data that never existed; **(ii) Falsification** — the manipulation of research data, materials, or processes such that the research is not accurately represented; and **(iii) Plagiarism** — the appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results, or words without giving due credit. Together, these are referred to in the literature as *FFP*.
*(Mark distribution: 1 definition + 1 each for 3 categories = 4 mapped to 3 marks, hence the 3rd category is brief.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Discuss the four-principle framework of bioethics proposed by Beauchamp and Childress. For each principle, give **one** concrete example of an ethical violation in biotechnology research.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Principlist Framework** of Beauchamp and Childress (1979) is the dominant ethical lens used in biomedical research, clinical medicine, and biotechnology governance. It comprises four prima facie principles:

**1. Respect for Autonomy (7-Mark Sub-component, ~1.5 marks):**
- *Definition:* Acknowledging the right of individuals to make informed, voluntary decisions about their own bodies, data, and participation.
- *Violation Example:* A clinical trial of a gene-therapy vector is conducted on tribal patients who were not informed, in a language they understand, that the trial is experimental. This is a textbook **informed-consent breach**.

**2. Beneficence (~1.5 marks):**
- *Definition:* The obligation to act in the best interest of the patient or subject — to do good.
- *Violation Example:* Researchers deliberately withhold a proven antiretroviral regimen from a control arm of an HIV vaccine trial in a developing country to test the experimental candidate — a clear **withholding-of-standard-care** violation.

**3. Non-maleficence (~1.5 marks):**
- *Definition:* The obligation to "do no harm" (*primum non nocere*).
- *Violation Example:* A laboratory working on a BSL-3 pathogen bypasses negative-pressure containment, exposing technicians and the surrounding community to aerosolized risk — a **biosafety violation**.

**4. Justice (~1.5 marks):**
- *Definition:* The fair, equitable distribution of benefits, risks, and burdens of research.
- *Violation Example:* A pharmaceutical company conducts all Phase I safety trials in a low-income country but markets the resulting drug exclusively in high-income nations — **exploitation and distributive injustice**.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Although the four principles are prima facie (each binding unless overridden), a violation of any one triggers formal institutional inquiry and potentially regulatory action.

---

**(b)** *Explain the institutional and national mechanisms available in India for addressing ethical violations in biomedical research. List **at least four** bodies.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

India has developed a **multi-tiered regulatory architecture** for the oversight of biomedical and biotechnological research ethics.

**1. Institutional Biosafety Committee (IBSC) (~1.5 marks):**
Established under the **Rules 1989 of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**, the IBSC operates at the level of every research institution handling genetically engineered organisms. It is the *first line of review* for recombinant DNA research and has the authority to halt non-compliant projects.

**2. Institutional Ethics Committee / Institutional Review Board (IEC / IRB) (~1.5 marks):**
Constituted under the **ICMR National Ethical Guidelines for Biomedical and Health Research Involving Human Participants (2017)**, the IEC reviews all human-subjects research protocols, consent procedures, and risk-benefit ratios.

**3. Review Committee on Genetic Manipulation (RCGM) (~1.5 marks):**
Functioning under the **Department of Biotechnology (DBT)**, the RCGM monitors safety aspects of ongoing research projects in genetic engineering and recombinant DNA products.

**4. Genetic Engineering Appraisal Committee (GEAC) (~1.5 marks):**
The apex body under the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)**, the GEAC is the statutory authority for approving large-scale use and commercial release of genetically engineered products, including GM crops and industrial enzymes.

**5. Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO) (~0.5 mark for additional body):**
The national regulator for clinical trials, medical devices, and biologics, operating under the **New Drugs and Clinical Trials Rules, 2019**.

**6. Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) (~0.5 mark):**
The apex scientific body that issues the *National Ethical Guidelines*, the *gold standard* reference for any research-ethics violation dispute in India.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Part B:** Students commonly *name-drop* regulatory bodies without explaining their *jurisdictional scope* or *the specific violation class they address*. The examiner expects you to map each body to a *tier of violation*. A generic list of acronyms earns 2-3 marks; a mapped, scoped explanation earns full 7 marks.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)**

**(a)** *What is plagiarism? Differentiate between **plagiarism**, **self-plagiarism**, and **duplicate publication**. Why is each considered a violation of research ethics?* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Plagiarism (~2.5 marks):**
Plagiarism is the appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results, or words without giving appropriate credit. It violates the principle of **Honesty** in research, undermines the originality of the scientific record, and constitutes **intellectual theft**. *Example:* Copying paragraphs from a published review article into one's own thesis without quotation or citation.

**2. Self-Plagiarism (~1.5 marks):**
Also called *text recycling*, self-plagiarism occurs when an author reuses substantial portions of their own previously published work without disclosure. It is unethical because it (a) misleads reviewers about the novelty of the submission, (b) violates copyright transfer agreements, and (c) artificially inflates the author's publication count. *Example:* Submitting the same methodology section, with minor rewording, across three different papers.

**3. Duplicate Publication (~1.5 marks):**
Duplicate publication is the publication of a paper that substantially overlaps with one already published by the same or different authors, without clear cross-referencing. It violates the principle of **Transparency** and wastes peer-review resources. It is also called *redundant publication*. *Example:* Splitting a single study into two minimal-unit papers (*salami slicing*) and submitting both simultaneously.

**4. Ethical Status (Concluding ~1.5 marks):**
All three are formally classified as **Research Misconduct** under the U.S. Office of Research Integrity (ORI) definition and under the COPE (Committee on Publication Ethics) guidelines. Each is a sanctionable offense, leading to retraction, institutional penalties, and potential long-term damage to the author's career.

---

**(b)** *With the help of a real or hypothetical case, illustrate how a violation of **informed consent** undermines bioethical principles. Suggest a preventive framework.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. The Case (Tuskegee-Inspired Hypothetical) (~1.5 marks):**
*Dr. P conducts a study on a coastal fishing community to test a new genetically modified probiotic for water-purification. The villagers are asked to drink the water but are told only that "a government health program is being tested."
