Phase 1 2 3 4"] CT --> M["Market Approval
DCGI FDA"] M --> E["Exclusive Marketing
Patent Term Active"] E -->|"Near Expiry"| G["Generic Entry
Section 84 CL"] G -->|"Patent Expired"| Pub["Public Domain
Knowledge Diffusion"] P -.->|"Opposition"| O["Pre Grant
Opposition"] P -.->|"Opposition"| O2["Post Grant
Opposition"] ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define Intellectual Property Rights. List the four statutory criteria for patentability in India.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Intellectual Property Rights are the legal rights granted to creators/inventors over intangible creations of the mind, providing exclusive commercial exploitation rights for a defined period. - **Four Criteria (2 marks):** 1. **Novelty** — not known in the public domain globally before the priority date 2. **Inventive Step** — not obvious to a person skilled in the art 3. **Industrial Application** — capable of being made or used in an industry 4. **Statutory Permissibility** — not excluded under Sections 3 and 4 of the Patents Act, 1970 ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **What is the role of TRIPS agreement in harmonizing global IP standards? Mention India's commitment regarding patent term.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **TRIPS Role (2 marks):** The Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (TRIPS) agreement, administered by the WTO (1995), sets **minimum standards** of IP protection across all member nations, eliminating trade-distorting IP practices and ensuring technology transfer to developing countries. It mandates protection for patents, copyrights, trademarks, GI, and undisclosed information. - **India's Commitment (1 mark):** India committed to a minimum **20-year** patent protection term for inventions, formalized in the **2005 amendment** to the Patents Act, 1970, opening product patents in pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals. --- ## Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Module 1, Choice Option **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Bank 2024]** **(a)** Explain in detail the various types of Intellectual Property Rights recognized in India. Discuss the salient features of the **Patents Act, 1970** with special reference to its **biotechnology-relevant amendments**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Critically analyze the **TRIPS agreement** and its implications for **pharmaceutical patents in India**, citing the **Novartis v. Union of India (2013)** case. Discuss how **Section 3(d)** balances innovation with public health. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Types of IPR in India (4 Marks):** | Type | Statute | Term | |---|---|---| | Patent | Patents Act, 1970 | 20 years | | Copyright | Copyright Act, 1957 | Life + 60 years | | Trademark | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | 10 years renewable | | Industrial Design | Designs Act, 2000 | 10 + 5 years | | Geographical Indication | GI Act, 1999 | 10 years renewable | | Plant Variety | PPV&FR Act, 2001 | 15–18 years | **Salient Features of Patents Act, 1970 with Biotech Amendments (3 Marks):** - **2002 Amendment** — permitted patenting of **microorganisms** (non-natural) - **2005 Amendment** — introduced **product patents** in food, drugs, and chemicals (TRIPS compliance) - **2005 Amendment** — added **Section 3(d)** to prevent evergreening - Provision for **compulsory licensing** under Section 84 - **Pre-grant** and **post-grant opposition** mechanisms #### Model Solution for (b): **TRIPS and Pharma Patents (3.5 Marks):** - TRIPS mandated WTO members to grant **20-year** product patents for pharmaceuticals from **1 January 2005**. - India availed the **transitional period** (1995–2005) to retain only process patents. - The **post-2005** regime increased generic competition concerns, especially for **HIV/AIDS, cancer, and hepatitis** drugs. **Novartis v. Union of India 2013 (3.5 Marks):** - The Supreme Court **refused to grant** a patent on the **beta-crystalline form of Imatinib Mesylate (Gleevec)**. - The Court held that: - The new form did not show **significantly enhanced therapeutic efficacy** over the known compound. - It was therefore a violation of **Section 3(d)**. - Mere improvements without superior efficacy amount to **evergreening**. - The judgment is hailed as a landmark for **patient rights** and **public health**, balancing innovation incentives with accessibility. ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice Option **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Bank 2024]** **(a)** Discuss the concept of **Geographical Indications (GI)** in India. Explain the registration procedure, the role of **GI Registry in Chennai**, and provide **two examples of bio-relevant Indian GI products**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights (PPV&FR) Act, 2001**. In what way does it differ from the **UPOV Convention**? Discuss its **bioethical significance** for Indian farmers. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Concept of GI (1.5 Marks):** A **Geographical Indication** is a sign used on products that have a specific geographical origin and possess qualities, reputation, or characteristics essentially attributable to that origin. Examples: **Darjeeling Tea, Basmati Rice, Alphonso Mango, Mysore Sandalwood, Kani Shawl**. **Registration Procedure (3 Marks):** 1. Application by a **producer association, organization, or authority** (not individual) to the **GI Registry, Chennai**. 2. Statement of case with evidence of distinctiveness. 3. **Examination** by the Registrar — 3 months for rectification of defects. 4. Publication in the **GI Journal** for **3 months** of opposition. 5. **Registration** granted if unopposed or if opposition fails. 6. Certificate issued — **10 years** validity, renewable. **Examples (2.5 Marks):** - **Darjeeling Tea** (first Indian GI, registered 2004) — unique muscatel flavor from Darjeeling district's elevation, climate, and *Camellia sinensis var. sinensis* cultivar. - **Mysore Sandalwood Oil** — high santalol content (≥ 90%) due to specific soil and climate of Karnataka. #### Model Solution for (b): **PPV&FR Act, 2001 (3 Marks):** - Provides for **plant breeders' rights** (PBR) and uniquely, **farmers' rights** (FR). - Establishes the **Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights Authority (PPV&FRA)** in New Delhi. - **Protection period:** 18 years for trees/vines; 15 years for other crops; 15 years for extant varieties. - A variety must be **DUS-tested:** **D**istinct, **U**niform, **S**table. **Difference from UPOV (2 Marks):** | Feature | UPOV 1991 | PPV&FR Act, 2001 | |---|---|---| | Farmers' right to save seed | Restricted (mostly prohibited) | **Permitted and protected** | | Compulsory licensing | Limited | **Yes, with conditions** | | Disclosure of source | Not mandatory | **Mandatory (Section 40)** | | Benefit sharing | Not provided | **Provided (Section 26)** | | Essentially derived varieties | Yes | Yes | **Bioethical Significance (2 Marks):** - Protects **traditional and indigenous varieties** developed over centuries by Indian farmers. - Empowers farmers to claim **benefit sharing** when their varieties are used in commercial breeding programs. - **Prevents biopiracy** of India's genetic heritage. - Preserves **agrobiodiversity** and food sovereignty. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:** > 1. **Confusing Patent Term with Copyright Term** — Patents = 20 years; Copyright = Life + 60 years. Many students write 60 years for patents. > 2. **Forgetting the 3-year compulsory licensing threshold** — Section 84 licenses can be sought ONLY after **3 years** from grant, not before. > 3. **Section 3(d) Misinterpretation** — Students often think Section 3(d) bars all pharma patents. It only bars new forms **without enhanced efficacy**. > 4. **Plant Variety vs. Patent Confusion** — In India, plant varieties are **NOT** protected by patents but by the **PPV&FR Act** (Article 27.3(b) of TRIPS flexibility). > 5. **TRIPS Effective Date for India** — Many students write 1995; the relevant date for pharma product patents is **1 January 2005**. --- ## Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — 30-Second Recall** - **Eight IPR Pillars** (P-T-C-D-G-S-P-S): Patent, Trademark, Copyright, Design, GI, Semiconductor, PPV&FR, Secret. - **Patent Term = 20 years** from priority date (not grant date). - **Patentability Tests = Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application + Not Excluded.** - **Section 3(d)** — new forms of known substances need **significantly enhanced efficacy** to be patentable. - **Copyright = automatic** (no registration needed); arises on creation. - **Trademark = first-to-use** (India) vs. first-to-file (USA). - **GI = 10 years renewable**; applied by producer association, not individual. - **PPV&FR = 15 years (crops), 18 years (trees)**; protects **farmers' rights** uniquely. - **TRIPS = WTO, 1995**; India product patent pharma from **1 Jan 2005**. - **Compulsory License threshold = 3 years** post-grant (Section 84). - **First Indian Compulsory License = Natco's Sorafenib (2012)**, royalty = **6%** of net sales. - **Novartis v. Union of India (2013)** — landmark Section 3(d) case for **Gleevec**. - **Traditional Knowledge** defeats novelty — Turmeric, Neem cases. - **Idea-Expression Dichotomy** — copyright protects expression, not idea. - **TRIPS Flexibilities** — compulsory licensing, parallel imports, Bolar exception. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme — Expected Question Weightage:** > - Module 1 (IPR) carries **~30%** of total marks in PEBBT522. > - Typical paper: **1 Part-A (3 marks)** + **1 Part-B (14 marks)** on this module. > - High-yield sub-topics: **Section 3(d)**, **TRIPS + Gleevec case**, **GI procedure**, **PPV&FR vs. UPOV**.

topics.title: Patenting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT522 - BIOETHICS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Patenting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: PATENTING — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Patent (Statutory Definition under the Indian Patents Act, 1970 — as amended in 2005):**
> A *patent* is an exclusive statutory right granted by the Government of the State to a true and first inventor (or his assignee/successor) for a limited period (currently **20 years** from the date of filing) in respect of an **invention**, provided the invention is **novel**, involves an **inventive step**, and is capable of **industrial application**. During this period, the patentee has the exclusive right to use, make, sell, or import the patented invention; no third party can exploit the invention commercially without the patentee's consent.

In the bioethics context, "patenting" refers specifically to the **legal protection of biotechnological inventions** — including genetically engineered organisms, biological processes, pharmaceutical formulations, diagnostic kits, isolated gene sequences, microbial strains, and bio-informatics tools — and the resulting **ethical, social, and legal implications** for humanity, biodiversity, and equitable access to healthcare.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe Copyright" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Chef's Secret Recipe**
> Imagine you invent an extraordinary new dish in your kitchen. It is unique, requires a clever twist in technique, and could be sold commercially. If you publish the recipe openly in a cookbook, any restaurant could copy it. So you approach the government, disclose the full recipe in a confidential document, and in return, the government grants you a **time-limited monopoly** (say, 20 years). During this window, only *you* (or those you license) can commercially serve that dish. After the term ends, the recipe becomes public domain — anyone may use it. This is exactly what a patent does for an invention.

In biotechnology, replace the "recipe" with a **genetic construct, a fermentation process, a monoclonal antibody, or a CRISPR delivery vector**. The patent system rewards inventors for public disclosure while protecting commercial interests — this is the central **quid pro quo** of patent law.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus-Aligned)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Invention** | A new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application. |
| **Discovery** | Mere finding of something pre-existing in nature — *not patentable* on its own. |
| **Prior Art** | All information made available to the public anywhere in the world before the filing date. |
| **Novelty** | The invention must not be anticipated by any prior art. |
| **Inventive Step** | The feature must not be obvious to a person skilled in the art (PSA). |
| **Industrial Application** | The invention must be capable of being made or used in an industry. |
| **Patent Specification** | The detailed legal-technical document filed describing the invention. |
| **Opposition** | A legal challenge against the grant of a patent, filed pre- or post-grant. |
| **Infringement** | Unauthorized making, using, selling, or importing the patented invention. |
| **Compulsory License** | Government-authorized use of a patent without the patentee's consent, in public interest. |
| **TRIPS** | Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights (WTO agreement, 1995). |
| **PCT** | Patent Cooperation Treaty (administered by WIPO). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Snapshot — Must Memorize:**
> - Patent term: **20 years** (from date of filing)
> - Renewal: Annually after grant
> - Territorial rights: Patent is valid **only in the country** where it is granted
> - First-to-file system (not first-to-invent) — India adopted this in 2003 amendment
> - Publication of application: **18 months** after priority date

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 1: PATENTING — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Principles of Patent Law

The patent system is built upon **four pillars of jurisprudence**. Understanding these pillars is critical for KTU Module-1 questions.

### Pillar 1: The Quid Pro Quo Doctrine
- Inventor **publicly discloses** the complete invention in exchange for a **time-limited monopoly**.
- This balances private reward with public knowledge dissemination.
- Bioethical tension: in pharma/biotech, the disclosure may not reduce prices if no generic competition arises.

### Pillar 2: Territoriality Principle
- A patent granted in India has **no automatic legal force** in the USA, EU, or Japan.
- Inventors must file separately in each country (or use the **PCT route**) to obtain multi-jurisdictional protection.
- For biotechnological inventions, this creates a fragmented global protection map.

### Pillar 3: Independence Principle
- Patents granted in different countries for the same invention are **legally independent** of each other.
- Invalidation in one country does not affect validity in another.

### Pillar 4: First-to-File Rule
- The person who **first files** the application (not first invents) is entitled to the patent.
- This was codified in India by the **Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005** (Section 6, substituted Section 7).

## 2.2 Patentability Criteria — The "Triple Test"

An invention, to be patentable under **Section 2(1)(j)** read with **Section 3** of the Indian Patents Act, must pass three tests:

1. **Novelty (Section 2(1)(l))** — Not anticipated by prior art, anywhere in the world, in any form (written, oral, use, or publication).
2. **Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja))** — Features that involve a technical advance over existing knowledge, not obvious to a Person Skilled in the Art (PSA).
3. **Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac))** — Capable of being made or used in an industry, agriculture, or services.

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 3 — Non-Patentable Inventions in India (Bio-Relevant Highlights):**
> - **Section 3(b):** Inventions contrary to public order, morality, or that cause serious prejudice to human, animal, or plant life/health.
> - **Section 3(c):** Discovery of any living thing or non-living substance occurring in nature.
> - **Section 3(d):** New forms of known substances (e.g., salts, esters, polymorphs) that do not significantly enhance efficacy — *this was the key provision in the famous Novartis v. Union of India (2013) case concerning Glivec (Imatinib Mesylate)*.
> - **Section 3(e):** Substance obtained by a mere admixture resulting in aggregation of properties.
> - **Section 3(f):** Mere arrangement or re-arrangement of known devices.
> - **Section 3(h):** Methods of agriculture or horticulture.
> - **Section 3(i):** Any process for treatment of humans/animals (medical, surgical, diagnostic, therapeutic) — *plant and animal treatment is excluded* but product is patentable.
> - **Section 3(j):** Plants and animals in whole or any part thereof (other than micro-organisms) and essentially biological processes — **but microbiological processes and products of microbiological processes ARE patentable**.

## 2.3 Types of Patents

| Type | Scope | Bio-Relevance Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Product Patent** | Protects the substance/article itself | A new monoclonal antibody, a recombinant plasmid, a novel mRNA sequence |
| **Process Patent** | Protects the method of manufacture | A new fermentation protocol, a purification method, a PCR technique |
| **Utility Model (Petty Patent)** | Short-term protection for incremental improvements | Minor bioprocess optimisation |
| **Design Patent** | Protects ornamental appearance | Aesthetic shape of a medical device housing |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 2.4 — Conceptual "Formulas" / Decision Rules for Patenting in Bioethics**

| Concept | Rule / Formula | Applicability / Note |
|---|---|---|
| Patent Term | $T_{patent} = 20 \text{ years}$ (from date of filing) | Renewed annually post-grant |
| Pre-Grant Opposition Window | $T_{oppose} = 6 \text{ months}$ from publication | Filed under Section 25(1) |
| Publication Period (Automatic) | $T_{publish} = 18 \text{ months}$ from priority date | Section 11A |
| Working Statement Due | $T_{work} = \text{annually, in Form 27}$ | Section 146 |
| Compulsory License Trigger | 3 years after grant OR if not worked / not available at reasonable price | Section 84 |
| First-to-File Priority Date | $D_{priority} = \text{earliest filing date}$ worldwide | Section 7 |
| Patentability Score (Conceptual) | $P_{score} = N + IS + IA \geq 3$ | All three criteria must be satisfied |
| Biopatent "Ethical Filter" | $E_{pass} = (3b=No) \land (3d=Efficacy++) \land (3j=Microbe\ only)$ | Sections 3(b), 3(d), 3(j) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> This decision framework is the cornerstone of every **Research & Development (R&D) Intellectual Property (IP) cell** in pharma, biotech, and agri-tech industries. Researchers in KTU's bioengineering departments regularly apply this matrix to file patents in India, the US, and EU. A wrong classification can result in patent rejection, loss of millions in R&D, and (in the case of biopatents) serious ethical violations.

## 2.5 Bioethical Dimensions of Patenting — The Five Ethical Dilemmas

1. **Patenting of Life Forms:** Can a gene, a cell line, or a transgenic mouse be "owned"? The *Harvard OncoMouse* (1988, US) and *Braggs v. USPTO* debates are landmark cases.
2. **Patenting of Human Genes:** The US Supreme Court case *Association for Molecular Pathology v. Myriad Genetics (2013)* ruled that naturally occurring DNA segments are products of nature and not patent-eligible; only cDNA is patentable.
3. **Access vs. Affordability:** Pharmaceutical patents (e.g., antiretrovirals, hepatitis-C drugs like Sofosbuvir) raise the question: should public health override patent rights? **Compulsory licensing** (Section 84, Indian Patent Act) is the legal remedy — India issued its first compulsory license in **Natco v. Bayer (2012)** for Nexavar.
4. **Biopiracy:** Unauthorised patenting of traditional knowledge, plant varieties, or genetic resources of indigenous communities. Examples: turmeric patent revocation (1995, USA), neem patent (1994–2000), Basmati rice patent (1997, RiceTec, USA).
5. **Blocking Research:** Patents on foundational research tools (e.g., CRISPR-Cas9) can block downstream academic and clinical research — the Doudna-Charpentier vs. Zhang patent dispute over CRISPR is illustrative.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 1: PATENTING — Section 3: Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure for Filing a Bio-Patent in India

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive 10-Stage Analytical Walkthrough — Patent Prosecution Process**
> (KTU high-weightage topic — frequently asked in ESE)

### **Stage 1: Invention Disclosure (Internal Documentation)**
The inventor documents the invention in a structured internal disclosure form. The form captures: (i) title; (ii) field of invention; (iii) background with prior art; (iv) problem statement; (v) detailed description with experimental data; (vi) best mode of working; and (vii) claims defining the legal boundary.

**Logic:** The disclosure acts as the legal evidence of conception and reduction to practice. It is **not yet public**.

### **Stage 2: Patentability Search (Prior-Art Analysis)**
A search is conducted across global databases — **Espacenet, PatentScope (WIPO), Google Patents, USPTO, INPASS (India)**, and importantly non-patent literature (PubMed, IEEE, ACS, ScienceDirect) to identify any prior publication that could anticipate the invention.

**Logic:** This determines whether the invention passes the **Novelty test**. If a single prior reference discloses the same invention with all essential features, the application is **dead on arrival**.

### **Stage 3: Drafting the Patent Specification**
Two types of specifications may be filed:

$$
\text{Provisional Specification} \rightarrow \text{Establishes priority date (no formal claims, 12 months to convert)}
$$

$$
\text{Complete Specification} \rightarrow \text{Full description + claims + abstract + drawings}
$$

The **claims** are the most critical part — they define the legal scope. For a biotech invention, claims typically include:

- **Independent claim:** Broadest claim covering the core invention.
- **Dependent claims:** Narrower claims adding specific technical features.

### **Stage 4: Filing the Application**
Application is filed at the **Indian Patent Office** (Kolkata, Mumbai, Delhi, or Chennai — based on jurisdiction of applicant).

- Filed under **Section 7** with Form 1, Form 2 (specification), Form 3 (declaration of inventorship), Form 5 (declaration of foreign filing), and Form 26 (power of attorney, if applicable).
- Each sheet of the specification must bear the applicant's signature.

**Logic:** The filing date establishes the **priority date** — this is the *date from which the clock starts* for novelty assessment and 20-year term.

### **Stage 5: Publication (Automatic, 18 Months)**
The application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** at **18 months** from the priority date (Section 11A). Applicants may request **early publication** (Form 9) for faster processing.

**Logic:** Publication makes the invention accessible to the public — a critical moment in the **quid pro quo doctrine**.

### **Stage 6: Pre-Grant Opposition (Section 25(1))**
Within **6 months** of publication (or before grant, whichever is later), any interested party can file a **Pre-Grant Opposition** on grounds including:
- Lack of novelty, inventive step, or industrial applicability
- Non-disclosure of information
- Wrongful obtainment
- Contravention of Section 3 (non-patentable subject matter)

**Logic:** This democratises the patent system — anyone can challenge.

### **Stage 7: Examination Request (Form 18)**
The applicant (or any other person) must file a **Request for Examination** within **48 months** of the priority date. The controller then refers the application to a patent examiner.

### **Stage 8: Substantive Examination (FER Cycle)**
The examiner issues the **First Examination Report (FER)** citing prior art. The applicant must respond within **6 months** (extendable to 12 months) by:

- Amending claims
- Arguing distinguishability from cited prior art
- Submitting experimental data

**Multiple FER cycles** may occur before the application is deemed ready for grant or refusal.

### **Stage 9: Grant of Patent**
Once accepted, the patent is **granted** and published in the Patent Office Journal. The patentee must pay **renewal fees** annually from the 3rd year to maintain the patent.

### **Stage 10: Post-Grant Opposition (Section 25(2))**
Within **12 months** of grant, any interested party can file a post-grant opposition. After this, the patent can only be challenged in court or through revocation petition (Section 64).

**Logic:** Post-grant opposition provides a second public review window — crucial in biopatents where prior art might emerge late.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Patenting Decision for Common Bio-Subjects

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Walkthrough — Applying the Triple Test and Section 3 Filters**

| Subject | Novel? | Inventive Step? | Industrial? | Section 3 Hurdle | Patentable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Naturally occurring human BRCA1 gene | N/A (pre-existing) | No (discovery) | N/A | 3(c) — discovery in nature | **NO** (per *Myriad v. AMP*, 2013) |
| cDNA version of BRCA1 | Yes (artificial) | Yes | Yes | None violated | **YES** |
| A new recombinant insulin | Yes | Yes | Yes | None | **YES** |
| A new salt of a known drug (no efficacy boost) | Yes | No | Yes | 3(d) | **NO** (per *Novartis v. UoI*, 2013) |
| A new salt of a known drug (efficacy >100% increase) | Yes | Yes | Yes | 3(d) satisfied | **YES** |
| A transgenic mouse (US practice) | Yes | Yes | Yes | 3(j) — *but India excludes higher animals* | **NO in India**, YES in US/EP |
| A novel microbial strain (e.g., Pseudomonas sp.) | Yes | Yes | Yes | 3(j) — microbe carve-out | **YES** |
| Method of treating cancer in humans | Yes | Yes | Yes | 3(i) — medical treatment process | **NO** (process) |
| A new anticancer drug compound | Yes | Yes | Yes | None | **YES** |
| Turmeric powder for wound healing (traditional) | Known publicly | No (anticipated) | Yes | 3, 25(1) | **NO** (Biopiracy — revoked) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering/Research Application:**
> This matrix is the **decision dashboard** used by KTU bio-engineering and biotechnology students/researchers when they are about to publish or commercialise research. Misclassifying a discovery as an invention can lead to patent rejection and wasted R&D budget.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Case Study: The Novartis v. Union of India (2013) Decision

**Background:** Novartis AG sought a patent for the beta-crystalline form of Imatinib Mesylate (Glivec), a drug used for chronic myeloid leukemia. The Indian Patent Office (Chennai) rejected the application under **Section 3(d)**.

**Steps in the Court's Reasoning:**

1. **Step 1 — Interpretation of Section 3(d):** The Court held that Section 3(d) was introduced to **prevent evergreening** — the practice of obtaining fresh patents on trivial variations of known drugs to extend the monopoly.
2. **Step 2 — Test of "Efficacy":** The Supreme Court ruled that the test of "significantly enhanced efficacy" must apply to the **new form** itself, not to the original molecule. The new form must demonstrate a therapeutic advantage.
3. **Step 3 — Comparative Evidence:** Novartis argued that the beta-crystalline form had improved flow properties and 30% better bioavailability. The Court ruled that **bioavailability ≠ therapeutic efficacy**.
4. **Step 4 — Final Verdict:** The patent was refused. This landmark decision upheld India's commitment to **public health over private monopoly** in pharmaceuticals.

**Ethical Lesson:** A patent must reward genuine innovation, not strategic legal manoeuvres. This is the **bioethical filter** of patent law.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 1: PATENTING — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Patent Prosecution Lifecycle

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.1 — Block-Level Architecture of Indian Patent Prosecution**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Invention Conception] --> B[Internal Disclosure]
    B --> C[Prior Art Search]
    C --> D{Novelty and Inventive Step Exist?}
    D -- No --> E[Abandon or Reformulate Invention]
    D -- Yes --> F[Draft Provisional or Complete Specification]
    F --> G[File at Indian Patent Office with Forms 1 to 26]
    G --> H[Priority Date Established]
    H --> I[Wait 18 Months for Publication]
    I --> J[Pre Grant Opposition Window of 6 Months]
    J --> K[Request for Examination Form 18]
    K --> L[Patent Examiner Issues FER]
    L --> M{Response to FER Acceptable?}
    M -- No --> N[Refused or Hearings]
    M -- Yes --> O[Amended Claims Accepted]
    O --> P[Grant of Patent Published in Journal]
    P --> Q[Post Grant Opposition Window of 12 Months]
    Q --> R[Annual Renewal Fees Payable]
    R --> S[Patent Enforced for 20 Year Term]
```

## 4.2 Patentability Decision Tree (Bioethical Filter)

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.2 — Decision Architecture for Bio-Patent Eligibility in India**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Invention in Bio Domain]) --> Q1{Is it a Discovery not an Invention?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Out1[Not Patentable Section 3 c]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is it Contrary to Public Order or Morality?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Out2[Not Patentable Section 3 b]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is it a New Form of Known Substance with no Efficacy Boost?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Out3[Not Patentable Section 3 d]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is it a Higher Plant or Animal or Essentially Biological Process?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Out4[Not Patentable Section 3 j]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Is it a Medical Treatment Method for Humans or Animals?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Out5[Process not Patentable Section 3 i]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Does it pass Novelty Inventive Step and Industrial Application?}
    Q6 -- No --> Out6[Rejected on Substantive Grounds]
    Q6 -- Yes --> Grant[Patent Granted under Section 43]
```

## 4.3 International Patent Filing Architecture (PCT Route)

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.3 — Block Diagram of the Patent Cooperation Treaty Filing Architecture**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Indian Inventor Files PCT Application at Receiving Office] --> B[WIPO International Bureau Acts as Receiving Office]
    B --> C[International Search Report ISR Generated]
    C --> D[Optional International Preliminary Examination]
    D --> E[National Phase Entry in Desired Countries at 30 Months from Priority Date]
    E --> F1[National Phase in India]
    E --> F2[National Phase in USPTO]
    E --> F3[National Phase in EPO]
    E --> F4[National Phase in Japan Patent Office]
    E --> F5[National Phase in Other WTO Member States]
    F1 --> G1[Indian Patent Office Examines and Grants]
    F2 --> G2[USPTO Examines and Grants]
    F3 --> G3[EPO Examines and Grants]
    F4 --> G4[Japan Patent Office Examines and Grants]
    F5 --> G5[Respective National Offices Examine and Grant]
```

## 4.4 Architecture of Bioethical Concerns in Patenting

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.4 — Block Diagram Mapping Bioethical Issues to Patenting Practices**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bioethical Concerns in Patenting] --> B[Patenting of Life Forms]
    A --> C[Access to Medicines and TRIPS Compliance]
    A --> D[Biopiracy and Traditional Knowledge]
    A --> E[Blocking of Research via Upstream Patents]
    A --> F[Privacy and Genetic Information]
    B --> B1[Gene Patents Myriad Case]
    B --> B2[Transgenic Animals OncoMouse]
    C --> C1[Compulsory Licensing Natco vs Bayer 2012]
    C --> C2[TRIPS Flexibilities and Doha Declaration 2001]
    D --> D1[Turmeric and Neem Patent Revocations]
    D --> D2[Nagoya Protocol on Access and Benefit Sharing]
    E --> E1[CRISPR Cas9 Patent Disputes]
    E --> E2[Hearing and Patent Thickets in Genomics]
    F --> F1[Genetic Data Ownership GDPR and HIPAA]
    F --> F2[Informed Consent in Biobanking]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# MODULE 1: PATENTING — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBBT522]`
> **Define a patent. Mention any two criteria of patentability.**

**Model Answer:**
A patent is an exclusive statutory right granted by the government to a true and first inventor for a limited period of **20 years** in respect of an invention, in exchange for public disclosure of the invention.

**Two criteria of patentability (out of the three):**
1. **Novelty:** The invention must be new, not anticipated by any prior art anywhere in the world.
2. **Inventive Step:** The invention must involve a technical feature that is not obvious to a person skilled in the art.
3. **Industrial Application:** The invention must be capable of being made or used in an industry.

(3 marks — definition 1 + any 2 criteria 1 each)

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEBBT522]`
> **Differentiate between discovery and invention in the context of patent law.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Discovery | Invention |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Finding of something pre-existing in nature | Creation of a new product or process |
| Patentability | **Not patentable** (Section 3(c) of the Indian Patents Act) | **Patentable** if it meets novelty, inventive step, and industrial application |
| Examples | A new gene isolated from a naturally occurring organism; a new plant species in the wild | A recombinant insulin molecule; a new fermentation process |
| Ethical Status | Often raises biopiracy concerns | Subject to ethical filters (Sections 3(b), 3(d), 3(j)) |

(3 marks — tabular difference with 1 example each)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **ESE Module 1 — Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), carrying **7 marks each**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEBBT522, Module 1]`
> **(a)** Explain the procedure for grant of a patent in India, with a neat flowchart. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the ethical issues involved in patenting of life forms and human genes, citing the *Myriad Genetics* and *Novartis v. Union of India* cases. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Answer to Q.A (a) — Procedure for Grant of a Patent**

The procedure for grant of a patent in India is governed by the **Indian Patents Act, 1970** (as amended in 2005). The sequential steps are:

1. **Filing of Application (Section 7):** The applicant files the application in the prescribed manner (Form 1, Form 2, Form 3, Form 5, Form 26) at the appropriate Patent Office. The filing date is the priority date.

2. **Publication (Section 11A):** The application is published in the Patent Office Journal after **18 months** from the priority date. Early publication can be requested via Form 9.

3. **Request for Examination (Section 11B):** Filed in Form 18 within **48 months** of the priority date.

4. **Examination:** The application is examined for compliance with Sections 2, 3, and the substantive criteria. The **First Examination Report (FER)** is issued to the applicant.

5. **Response to FER:** The applicant responds with amendments, arguments, and experimental data within 6 months (extendable to 12 months).

6. **Pre-Grant Opposition (Section 25(1)):** Any person may oppose within 6 months of publication.

7. **Grant of Patent (Section 43):** If accepted, the patent is granted and published. The patentee must pay renewal fees annually.

8. **Post-Grant Opposition (Section 25(2)):** Any person may oppose within 12 months of grant.

(7 marks: 5 marks for procedural explanation, 2 marks for flowchart/diagram)

#### **Model Answer to Q.A (b) — Ethical Issues in Patenting Life Forms and Human Genes**

**Patenting of Life Forms:**
- The landmark case is the **Harvard OncoMouse (1988)** — the first animal patented in the US. It raised ethical questions about commodification of sentient life.
- In India, **Section 3(j)** excludes plants and animals (other than microorganisms) and essentially biological processes from patentability, reflecting a more restrictive ethical stance.

**Patenting of Human Genes — Myriad Genetics (2013):**
- The US Supreme Court in *Association for Molecular Pathology v. Myriad Genetics* ruled that **naturally occurring DNA segments are products of nature and not patentable**.
- However, **complementary DNA (cDNA)**, being synthetically created, is patent-eligible.
- **Ethical Implication:** Allows diagnostic companies to hold exclusive rights on BRCA1/BRCA2 breast cancer tests, raising concerns about cost and access to genetic testing.

**Novartis v. Union of India (2013) — Glivec Case:**
- Novartis sought a patent on the beta-crystalline form of Imatinib Mesylate.
- The Supreme Court rejected the application under **Section 3(d)**, ruling that the new form did not demonstrate significantly enhanced therapeutic efficacy.
- **Ethical Implication:** Prevents **evergreening** — the practice of extending patent monopolies through trivial modifications — and supports the right to affordable medicines in developing countries.

(7 marks: 2 marks for life forms, 3 marks for Myriad, 2 marks for Novartis)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **Q.B.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEBBT522, Module 1]`
> **(a)** Discuss the criteria of patentability under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. What are the non-patentable inventions under Section 3? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **biopiracy** with two case studies. Discuss India's response through the **Nagoya Protocol** and **Traditional Knowledge Digital Library (TKDL)**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Answer to Q.B (a) — Criteria of Patentability and Section 3 Exclusions**

**Criteria of Patentability (Section 2(1)(j)):**
An invention is patentable if it satisfies the following:
1. **Novelty (Section 2(1)(l)):** The invention must not be anticipated by any prior art, anywhere in the world, in any form.
2. **Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja)):** It must involve a technical advance or have economic significance, and must not be obvious to a Person Skilled in the Art (PSA).
3. **Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac)):** It must be capable of being made or used in an industry, agriculture, or services.

**Non-Patentable Inventions (Section 3) — Bio-Relevant Highlights:**
- **3(b):** Inventions contrary to public order or morality; harmful to life/health.
- **3(c):** Mere discovery of a living thing or non-living substance occurring in nature.
- **3(d):** New forms of known substances (salts, esters, polymorphs, etc.) without enhanced efficacy.
- **3(e):** Substances obtained by mere admixture.
- **3(f):** Mere rearrangement of known devices.
- **3(h):** Methods of agriculture or horticulture.
- **3(i):** Methods of treatment of humans/animals (medical, surgical, diagnostic, therapeutic).
- **3(j):** Plants and animals (except microorganisms) and essentially biological processes; microbiological processes ARE patentable.
- **3(k):** Mathematical or business methods; schemes or rules; mental acts.

(7 marks: 3 marks for criteria, 4 marks for Section 3)

#### **Model Answer to Q.B (b) — Biopiracy and India's Response**

**Concept of Biopiracy:**
Biopiracy refers to the **unauthorised patenting and commercial exploitation of biological resources, traditional knowledge, and genetic material** derived from indigenous communities and biodiversity-rich countries, typically by corporations or researchers from industrialised nations, without prior informed consent and without equitable benefit-sharing.

**Case Study 1: Turmeric Patent (1995–1997)**
- The University of Mississippi Medical Center (USA) was granted a patent in 1995 for the use of turmeric in wound healing.
- India's Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) filed a challenge citing **prior art** — turmeric has been used in Indian Ayurvedic medicine for centuries.
- The US Patent Office **revoked the patent in 1997**, marking a landmark victory against biopiracy.

**Case Study 2: Neem Patent (1994–2000)**
- The European Patent Office granted a patent to W.R. Grace and the US Department of Agriculture for the fungicidal properties of neem (Azadirachta indica).
- India and the NGO "Research Foundation for Science, Technology and Ecology" (Vandana Shiva) filed a challenge.
- The EPO **revoked the patent in 2000** on grounds of prior art and lack of novelty.

**India's Response:**

1. **Nagoya Protocol on Access and Benefit Sharing (2010):** An international agreement that ensures fair and equitable sharing of benefits arising from the use of genetic resources. India is a signatory.

2. **Traditional Knowledge Digital Library (TKDL):** A collaborative initiative by CSIR and the Ministry of AYUSH that has documented over **390,000** traditional medicinal formulations in a digital, patent-database-compatible format. It has successfully prevented biopiracy attempts by acting as a defensive prior art resource.

3. **Biological Diversity Act, 2002:** Provides a legal framework for access to biological resources and traditional knowledge, with prior informed consent and benefit sharing.

(7 marks: 2 marks for concept, 2 marks for case studies, 3 marks for India's response)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in Patenting questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Discovery" with "Invention"** — Section 3(c) excludes mere discoveries. Always quote the section.
> 2. **Forgetting the 20-year term** — Examiners check this exact figure. Writing 15 years or 25 years is a **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Skipping the 18-month publication period** — This is a frequently asked sub-question. Memorise Section 11A.
> 4. **Missing the Section 3(d) "efficacy" nuance** — In pharma/chemistry questions, simply saying "new salt is patentable" is incomplete. Always tie it to "significantly enhanced therapeutic efficacy."
> 5. **Ignoring bioethical angles** — KTU specifically tests the **bioethics** angle. Always pair a legal answer with the **ethical implication** (e.g., "this prevents evergreening" or "this protects public health").
> 6. **Using outdated terminology** — "First-to-invent" is incorrect since 2003 (now first-to-file). Penalty: 1 mark.
> 7. **Not citing sections** — Vague answers like "a patent protects inventions" are penalised. Quote **Section 2(1)(j)**, **Section 3(d)**, **Section 25(1)**, etc.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Patenting Module (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Patent definition** — Statutory right, 20-year term, quid pro quo doctrine.
- [ ] **Patentability Triple Test** — Novelty + Inventive Step + Industrial Application.
- [ ] **First-to-File Rule** (Section 7) — India adopted in 2003.
- [ ] **Section 3 Non-Patentable Categories** — Memorise at least 3(b), 3(c), 3(d), 3(i), 3(j).
- [ ] **Section 3(d) Novartis Case (2013)** — Prevents evergreening, requires "significantly enhanced efficacy."
- [ ] **Myriad Genetics (2013, US)** — Naturally occurring DNA not patentable; cDNA is.
- [ ] **18-month publication period** — Section 11A.
- [ ] **Pre-grant opposition (6 months) vs. Post-grant opposition (12 months).**
- [ ] **48-month deadline** for Request for Examination (Section 11B).
- [ ] **PCT route** — Allows 30-month national phase entry in member countries.
- [ ] **Biopiracy cases** — Turmeric (1997 revoked), Neem (2000 revoked), Basmati (1997 RiceTec).
- [ ] **TKDL** — Defensive prior art repository of 390,000+ traditional formulations.
- [ ] **Nagoya Protocol (2010)** — Access and benefit sharing (ABS) for genetic resources.
- [ ] **Compulsory License (Section 84)** — Natco v. Bayer (2012) for Nexavar; 3-year post-grant trigger.
- [ ] **TRIPS Agreement (1995)** — WTO framework, 20-year minimum patent term.
- [ ] **Bioethical Principles in Patenting** — Autonomy (consent in biopiracy), Justice (access to medicines), Non-maleficence (no harm to biodiversity), Beneficence (innovation for public good).
- [ ] **Drafts and Forms** — Form 1 (Application), Form 2 (Specification), Form 5 (Foreign filing), Form 18 (Examination), Form 27 (Working statement).
- [ ] **Working statement** — Annual filing under Section 146, Form 27.
- [ ] **Territoriality** — A patent is country-specific.
- [ ] **Microorganisms ARE patentable** in India (carve-out in Section 3(j)); higher plants and animals are NOT.

**End of Module 1 — Patenting Notes.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Patent Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT522 - BIOETHICS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Patent Laws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Patent Laws

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

A **Patent** is a statutory right granted by the sovereign government to an inventor, conferring upon the patentee an exclusive privilege to make, use, sell, manufacture, or import the patented invention for a limited period of **20 years** from the date of filing, in exchange for the public disclosure of the invention.

**Patent Law** is the body of statutory provisions, judicial precedents, administrative rules, and international treaty obligations that govern the grant, enforcement, transfer, revocation, and infringement of patents. In the Indian context, patent law is principally codified under **The Patents Act, 1970** (as amended in 1999, 2002, and 2005 to become TRIPS-compliant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (High-Weightage for KTU)**
> A patent is a *territorial right* — it is enforceable only within the jurisdiction of the country that granted it. There is **no such thing as a "world patent"** under the current international legal regime.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Learning Outcome Mapping (CO1)**
> Students must be able to *describe* the framework of patent laws, distinguish between patentable and non-patentable subject matter, and explain the procedure for patent grant in India and internationally — with a specific focus on **biopatents** and **bioethical implications** relevant to life sciences and biotechnology.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Real Estate" Analogy

Imagine you have invented a unique machine that converts kitchen waste into biogas. If you simply *use* it, anyone can copy it, and you earn nothing. But if you **patent** it, the government gives you a "deed" — like a property deed for a house — that legally prevents others from making, selling, or using your machine for 20 years.

**Key analogies in plain English:**

| Patent Concept | Real-World Analogy | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| Patent | Property deed | Grants exclusive ownership |
| Patent Term (20 years) | Lease period | Time-limited monopoly |
| Patent Specification | Blueprint / Recipe | Public disclosure in return for rights |
| Infringement | Trespassing | Unauthorized use is illegal |
| Prior Art | Previous tenants | Existing knowledge kills novelty |
| Patent Office | Land Registry | Government authority granting rights |
| Renewal Fee | Property Tax | Pay to keep the right alive |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for remembering Patent = "NICE"**
> - **N** — Novelty (must be new)
> - **I** — Inventive Step (non-obvious)
> - **C** — Capable of Industrial Application (useful)
> - **E** — Exclusivity (territorial right for 20 years)

## 1.3 Genesis and Historical Evolution of Patent Laws

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **1474** | Venetian Statute | World's first codified patent law |
| **1624** | Statute of Monopolies (England) | Laid foundation of modern patent system |
| **1790** | US Patent Act | First US patent statute |
| **1856** | Indian Patents & Designs Act | First Indian patent law |
| **1883** | Paris Convention | First international IP treaty |
| **1970** | Indian Patents Act, 1970 | Independent Indian statute |
| **1970** | Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) | Unified international filing |
| **1994** | TRIPS Agreement | WTO-mandated global IP standards |
| **1999** | Indian Patents (Amendment) | Mail-box & exclusive marketing rights |
| **2005** | Indian Patents (Amendment) | Product patents in pharma & agro-chemicals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 2005 is Pivotal for India:**
> The 2005 amendment transformed India from a *process-patent* regime (only the manufacturing process was protected) into a *product-patent* regime (the end product itself is protected). This was a direct compliance obligation under TRIPS Article 65.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Patentability (Section 2 of Indian Patents Act, 1970)

For an invention to be granted a patent in India, it must satisfy three statutory criteria simultaneously. The failure of even one criterion renders the invention non-patentable.

### 2.1.1 Pillar 1 — Novelty (Section 2(1)(j))

An invention is "novel" if it has **not been anticipated by prior publication, prior use, or prior knowledge** anywhere in the world before the date of filing.

> **"Anticipation"** — Any single prior art reference that discloses every element of the claimed invention in the same combination constitutes novelty-destroying prior art.

### 2.1.2 Pillar 2 — Inventive Step (Section 2(1)(ja))

The invention must involve a **technical advance** as compared to existing knowledge, OR must have **economic significance**, AND must not be obvious to a person skilled in the art (PHOSITA).

> The **PHOSITA Test** is the legally recognized standard: would a hypothetical "person having ordinary skill in the art" find the invention obvious given the prior art?

### 2.1.3 Pillar 3 — Industrial Application (Section 2(1)(ac))

The invention must be capable of being **made or used in an industry** — a remarkably low bar. Almost any tangible product or process qualifies.

## 2.2 Patentable vs. Non-Patentable Subject Matter (Section 3 & 4)

### 2.2.1 PATENTABLE Subject Matter (Inventions)

- New products or processes with technical application
- Chemical compounds (including pharmaceuticals, post-2005)
- Biotechnological inventions (microorganisms, bioprocesses)
- Improved versions of known devices
- Software-embodied inventions (when coupled with hardware)

### 2.2.2 NON-PATENTABLE Subject Matter (Section 3 & 4 Exclusions)

| Section | Exclusion | Bioethics Connection |
|---|---|---|
| **3(a)** | Frivolous inventions, contrary to law/morality | Cloning of humans, bioweapons |
| **3(b)** | Discoveries, scientific principles | Natural law of gravity, E = mc² |
| **3(c)** | Mere discovery of new property/use | New medicinal use of turmeric |
| **3(d)** | Mere admixture / rearrangement | Combinations without synergistic effect |
| **3(e)** | Food, medicine, drug (composition known) | Disallowed under product-patent era |
| **3(f)** | Substances obtained by admixture | Routine formulations |
| **3(h)** | Methods of agriculture/horticulture | Conventional farming methods |
| **3(i)** | Methods of treatment of animals/humans | Surgery, therapy, diagnostic methods |
| **3(j)** | Plants/animals (essentially biological) | Traditional varieties |
| **3(k)** | Computer programs per se | Pure algorithms |
| **3(pa)** | Topography of integrated circuits | Already covered by separate law |
| **4** | Atomic energy, nuclear processes | Strategic sector |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Mistake:**
> "Computer programs" are NOT absolutely non-patentable in India. Only computer programs **per se** (i.e., as abstract algorithms) are excluded. When combined with a novel technical effect (e.g., a hardware-coupled AI diagnostic system), the invention IS patentable.

## 2.3 Types of Patent Applications in India

| Type | Section | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Ordinary Application** | Sec 7(1)(a) | No priority claim, no 18-month publication deferment |
| **Convention Application** | Sec 135 | Claims priority from a Paris Convention member country (12 months) |
| **PCT National Phase Application** | Sec 135 | Filed under Patent Cooperation Treaty, 31-month deadline |
| **Provisional Specification** | Sec 9(1) | Establishes priority date, 12 months to file complete spec |
| **Complete Specification** | Sec 9(2) | Full technical disclosure with claims |
| **Divisional Application** | Sec 16 | When one application claims multiple inventions |
| **Patent of Addition** | Sec 54 | Improvement/modification of parent invention |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield "Formula" Sheet (Statutory Periods & Numerical Standards)

> [!NOTE]
> Patent law has very few "formulas" but many **statutory time limits** that are heavily tested in KTU exams. The following table consolidates every numerical value you must memorize.

| Statutory Metric | Value | Statutory Authority |
|---|---|---|
| **Patent Term** | **20 years** from date of filing | Section 53(1) |
| Paris Convention Priority Period | **12 months** | Article 4C, Paris Convention |
| PCT International Phase Entry | **30/31 months** from priority date | PCT Article 22/39 |
| Provisional to Complete Spec | **12 months** | Section 9(1) |
| Publication (Automatic) | **18 months** from priority date | Section 11A |
| Request for Examination | Within **48 months** of filing | Section 11B(2) |
| First Examination Report (FER) issuance | Within **6 months** of RFE | Patent Rules, 2003 |
| Response to FER | **6 months** (extendable to 12) | Rule 24B |
| Renewal — start year | **3rd year** onwards | Section 53(2) |
| Renewal fee deadline | **6 months** before anniversary of filing date | Rule 80 |
| Working of patent disclosure | Required in Form 27 every fiscal | Section 146(2) |
| Compulsory License application | After **3 years** of grant | Section 84(1) |
| Opposition period (pre-grant) | **6 months** from publication OR before grant | Section 25(1) |
| Post-grant opposition | Within **12 months** of grant | Section 25(2) |
| Restoration of lapsed patent | Within **18 months** of cessation | Section 60(1) |

### 2.4.1 Critical Renewal Fee Math (Board-Favourite Calculation)

The renewal fees follow a graduated escalating structure. The total cost of maintaining a patent over 20 years is the **sum of annual renewal fees** plus a 6-month extension surcharge if delayed.

$$\text{Total Patent Maintenance Cost} = \sum_{y=3}^{20} F_y + \sum_{y=3}^{20} S_y \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{lapsed}}$$

where $F_y$ is the renewal fee for year $y$, $S_y$ is the late surcharge, and $\mathbb{1}_{\text{lapsed}}$ is the indicator function (1 if renewal was missed, 0 otherwise).

> [!TIP]
> **Board Trick:** Renewal fees are **not** due for the 1st and 2nd year after filing. The 3rd-year renewal must be paid *before* the 3rd anniversary of the filing date. This is a **favourite 3-mark question** in KTU exams.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Patent law is the **economic engine** of the global biotechnology and pharmaceutical industries:

- **Pharma R&D**: Patents enable recovery of the $1–2 billion cost of bringing a new drug to market.
- **Biotech Startups**: Patent portfolios are often the principal asset on balance sheets (IP valuation).
- **Academic Research**: Universities like IITs generate substantial revenue through licensing patented technologies (e.g., IIT Bombay's cancer-detection biosensor patent).
- **Technology Transfer**: Patents facilitate cross-border licensing between MNCs and Indian generic manufacturers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Patent Term Calculation

The statutory patent term is fixed by Section 53(1). However, the *effective* term depends on the difference between the **filing date** and the **grant date**. Derivation:

$$\text{Effective Patent Term} = T_{\text{filing date}} + 20 \text{ years} - (T_{\text{grant date}} - T_{\text{filing date}})$$

$$\text{Effective Patent Term} = 20 \text{ years}$$

> The statute guarantees exactly 20 years from filing, irrespective of how long examination takes. This is why strategic use of **provisional specifications** and **continuation applications** (in jurisdictions that allow them) is critical.

Let us compute the **effective term of protection** for a hypothetical drug filed on 1st January 2010 and granted on 1st July 2013.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{filing}} &= \text{01-Jan-2010} \\
T_{\text{grant}} &= \text{01-Jul-2013} \\
\Delta t_{\text{pendency}} &= 3.5 \text{ years} \\
\text{Patent expiry} &= T_{\text{filing}} + 20 \text{ years} \\
&= \text{01-Jan-2010} + 20 \text{ years} \\
&= \text{01-Jan-2030} \\
\text{Effective monopoly period} &= 20 \text{ years} - \Delta t_{\text{pendency}} \\
&= 20 - 3.5 = 16.5 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

The applicant thus enjoys 16.5 years of *post-grant* exclusivity, even though the statutory term is 20 years.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Patentability Decision Tree

The complete patentability determination can be expressed as a **conjunctive Boolean evaluation**:

$$P(I) = N(I) \land S(I) \land A(I) \land \neg E(I)$$

where:
- $N(I)$ = Novelty predicate (True if $I$ has no prior art)
- $S(I)$ = Inventive Step predicate (True if non-obvious to PHOSITA)
- $A(I)$ = Industrial Application predicate (True if industrially useful)
- $E(I)$ = Exclusion predicate (True if $I$ falls within Section 3 or 4)
- $I$ = the invention under examination
- $P(I)$ = Patentability outcome (True = patentable)

> A patent is granted **if and only if** all four predicates evaluate as required.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Patent Application Procedure in India

This is the most frequently asked 7-mark derivation question in KTU examinations. Each step must be memorized in order.

| Step | Stage | Statutory Basis | Time-Bound |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Prior Art Search** | Not statutory, but mandatory | Anytime before filing |
| 2 | **File Provisional Specification** | Section 9(1) | Optional but recommended |
| 3 | **File Complete Specification** | Section 9(2) | Within 12 months of provisional |
| 4 | **Publication in Patent Journal** | Section 11A | 18 months from priority date |
| 5 | **Request for Examination (RFE)** | Section 11B | Within 48 months of filing |
| 6 | **First Examination Report (FER)** | Rule 24B | Within 6 months of RFE |
| 7 | **Reply to FER with amendments** | Section 15 | Within 6 months (extendable to 12) |
| 8 | **Hearing (if objections persist)** | Section 14 | Scheduled by Controller |
| 9 | **Grant & Publication** | Section 43 | Within 7 days of grant |
| 10 | **Renewals begin** | Section 53(2) | From 3rd year onwards |

### 3.3.1 Detailed Mathematical Formulation of Examination Pendency

Let us model the *total time to grant* as the sum of stage-wise intervals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{grant}} &= T_0 + \Delta t_{\text{filing}} + \Delta t_{\text{publication}} + \Delta t_{\text{RFE}} + \Delta t_{\text{FER}} + \Delta t_{\text{reply}} + \Delta t_{\text{hearing}} \\
\text{where} \quad T_0 &= \text{Date of filing} \\
\Delta t_{\text{publication}} &= 18 \text{ months (automatic)} \\
\Delta t_{\text{RFE}} &\leq 48 \text{ months (applicant's discretion)} \\
\Delta t_{\text{FER}} &\leq 6 \text{ months from RFE} \\
\Delta t_{\text{reply}} &\leq 12 \text{ months (with extension)} \\
\Delta t_{\text{hearing}} &\leq 12 \text{ months from petition}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Types of Intellectual Property

Patent law is one branch of the larger Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) ecosystem. KTU students frequently confuse these — the following table resolves all ambiguity.

| IP Type | Subject Matter | Term | Key Statute | Indian Office |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Patent** | Invention (product/process) | 20 years | Patents Act, 1970 | Patent Office (Kolkata HQ) |
| **Trademark** | Brand identifier (mark, logo) | 10 years (renewable) | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | Registry of TM, Mumbai |
| **Copyright** | Literary, artistic, musical works | Life + 60 years | Copyright Act, 1957 | Copyright Office, Delhi |
| **Industrial Design** | Aesthetic features of an article | 15 years (10 + 5) | Designs Act, 2000 | Patent Office, Kolkata |
| **Geographical Indication** | Origin-linked product (Basmati, Darjeeling) | 10 years (renewable) | GI Act, 1999 | GI Registry, Chennai |
| **Trade Secret** | Confidential business information | Unlimited (if kept secret) | No statute; common law | — |
| **Plant Variety** | New, distinct, stable plant cultivar | 18–24 years | PPVFR Act, 2001 | PPVFR Authority, Delhi |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Bioethics Distinction:**
> A **biopatent** is granted for a *biotechnological invention* (e.g., a recombinant DNA construct, a monoclonal antibody, a genetically engineered microbe). It is **distinct** from a **plant variety protection** right (granted for new plant cultivars under the PPVFR Act). Confusing these is a routine KTU mark-deduction pitfall.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Patent Term Calculation

> **Question:** A pharmaceutical company files a complete patent specification on 15th March 2018. The application is published on 15th September 2019 (18 months later). The RFE is filed on 14th March 2022. The FER is issued on 14th September 2022. The applicant responds on 14th March 2023. The patent is granted on 30th June 2023. On what date will the patent expire?

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{filing}} &= \text{15-Mar-2018} \\
\text{Term} &= 20 \text{ years (from filing date, per Section 53(1))} \\
T_{\text{expiry}} &= T_{\text{filing}} + 20 \text{ years} \\
&= \text{15-Mar-2018} + 20 \text{ years} \\
&= \text{15-Mar-2038}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** The patent will expire on **15th March 2038**, regardless of the pendency period (5 years and 3.5 months in this case).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Patent Application Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Prior Art Search] --> B[Step 2: File Provisional Specification]
    B --> C{Within 12 months?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Step 3: File Complete Specification]
    C -- No --> E[Application deemed abandoned]
    D --> F[Step 4: Publication in Patent Journal - 18 months]
    F --> G{Request for Examination within 48 months?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Step 5: First Examination Report FER - 6 months]
    G -- No --> E
    H --> I{Reply to FER within 6 to 12 months?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Step 6: Hearing before Controller]
    I -- No --> K[Application Refused]
    J --> L{Controller Satisfied?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Step 7: Patent Granted and Published]
    L -- No --> K
    M --> N[Step 8: Renewal Fees from Year 3 to Year 20]
    N --> O[Patent Expiry after 20 years from filing]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Map — Types of Intellectual Property Rights

```mermaid
graph LR
    IPR[Intellectual Property Rights] --> IP1[Patent]
    IPR --> IP2[Trademark]
    IPR --> IP3[Copyright]
    IPR --> IP4[Industrial Design]
    IPR --> IP5[Geographical Indication]
    IPR --> IP6[Trade Secret]
    IPR --> IP7[Plant Variety Protection]

    IP1[Patent - 20 yrs - Inventions] --> S1[Product Patent]
    IP1 --> S2[Process Patent]
    IP1 --> S3[Biopatent - Biotech Inventions]

    IP3[Copyright - Life plus 60 yrs] --> C1[Literary]
    IP3 --> C2[Artistic]
    IP3 --> C3[Musical]
    IP3 --> C4[Software Code]

    IP5[GI - 10 yrs renewable] --> G1[Darjeeling Tea]
    IP5 --> G2[Basmati Rice]
    IP5 --> G3[Banarasi Silk]

    style IPR fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style IP1 fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style IP3 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#FFA07A,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Patentability Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Is the subject matter an invention?] --> Q2{Does it fall under Section 3 or 4 exclusion?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[NOT PATENTABLE]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3[Is it Novel? Section 2 1 j]
    Q3 -- No --> R1
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4[Does it involve Inventive Step? Section 2 1 ja]
    Q4 -- No --> R1
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Is it Industrially Applicable? Section 2 1 ac]
    Q5 -- No --> R1
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[PATENT GRANTED - Section 43]
    Q6 --> Q7[Right to exclude others for 20 years]

    style R1 fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFF
    style Q6 fill:#51CF66,color:#FFF
    style Q7 fill:#FFD700,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — International Patent Filing Routes

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Indian Applicant] --> B[Direct National Filing - India]
    A --> C[Paris Convention Route]
    A --> D[PCT Route]

    C --> C1[File in Country X first]
    C1 --> C2[Claim priority in India within 12 months]

    D --> D1[File PCT Application - WIPO Geneva]
    D1 --> D2[International Search Report - 16 months]
    D2 --> D3[National Phase Entry - 30 or 31 months]
    D3 --> D4[Multiple countries - USA, EU, Japan, India]

    B --> E[Patent Office Kolkata HQ]
    style A fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style B fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style D fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:**
> When asked to "draw/describe the patent filing process" in a 7-mark question, always include **all 8 steps** with their statutory section numbers and time limits. Missing a section number = -2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
> **Define a patent. List the three essential criteria for an invention to be patentable under the Indian Patents Act, 1970.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **patent** is a statutory right granted by the government to an inventor, conferring the exclusive privilege to make, use, sell, or import the patented invention for a limited period of 20 years from the date of filing, in exchange for full public disclosure of the invention.

The three essential criteria for patentability (Section 2, Indian Patents Act, 1970) are:

1. **Novelty** (Section 2(1)(j)) — The invention must not have been anticipated by any prior art, public use, or knowledge anywhere in the world before the filing date.

2. **Inventive Step** (Section 2(1)(ja)) — The invention must involve a technical advance or economic significance and must not be obvious to a person skilled in the art (PHOSITA).

3. **Industrial Application** (Section 2(1)(ac)) — The invention must be capable of being made or used in an industry.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for two criteria, 1 mark for the third criterion.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
> **Distinguish between product patent and process patent. What change was brought in by the 2005 amendment?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Product Patent | Process Patent |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject of protection** | The end product itself | The manufacturing process |
| **Coverage** | Prevents others from making, using, selling that specific product | Prevents others from using the same process to produce the product |
| **Reverse engineering** | Infringement even if competitor uses different process | Permitted — if different process is used, no infringement |

**2005 Amendment Impact:** The Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005 introduced **product patents** in the fields of pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals, replacing the earlier process-patent-only regime. This was a TRIPS-compliance obligation under Article 65. As a result, India granted its first product patent for the anti-cancer drug **Imatinib mesylate (Gleevec)** to Novartis, ending the long-standing compulsory licensing for life-saving drugs.

*(Examiner's Note: 2 marks for the table; 1 mark for the 2005 amendment.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the procedure for grant of a patent in India with reference to the relevant sections of the Patents Act, 1970.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various grounds on which a patent application may be opposed under Section 25 of the Patents Act.

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

The **patent grant procedure** in India comprises the following sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Prior Art Search (Pre-filing)**
Although not statutorily mandated, a thorough prior art search in patent and non-patent databases (Indian Patent Database, Espacenet, Google Patents) is performed to ensure novelty.

**Step 2 — Filing of Application (Section 7)**
The applicant files either a **provisional specification** (Section 9(1)) or a **complete specification** (Section 9(2)) with the Patent Office. Provisional filing establishes a priority date.

**Step 3 — Publication (Section 11A)**
The application is **automatically published** in the Patent Journal **18 months** after the priority date. Early publication can be requested (Form 9).

**Step 4 — Request for Examination (Section 11B)**
A formal RFE must be filed by the applicant (or any interested person) within **48 months** of filing. If no RFE is filed, the application is deemed withdrawn.

**Step 5 — First Examination Report (Section 12 & Rule 24B)**
The Controller issues the FER within **6 months** of RFE, identifying objections to novelty, inventive step, industrial application, or Section 3/4 exclusions.

**Step 6 — Response to FER (Section 15)**
The applicant must respond to the FER within **6 months** (extendable to 12 months) with written submissions and/or amendments to the claims.

**Step 7 — Hearing (Section 14)**
If objections persist, the Controller schedules a hearing for oral arguments. The applicant has up to 12 months to request the hearing.

**Step 8 — Grant and Publication (Section 43)**
On satisfaction, the patent is granted, entered in the Register of Patents, and published in the Patent Journal. The patentee receives a Certificate of Patent.

**Step 9 — Renewal (Section 53(2))**
Renewal fees are payable from the **3rd year** onwards.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark per step, 1 mark for statutory section reference, bonus mark for time limits.)*

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Grounds for Opposition under Section 25 of the Patents Act, 1970:**

**Section 25(1) — Pre-Grant Opposition** (any time from publication to grant)
1. Wrongful obtainment of the invention
2. Prior publication in India or abroad
3. Anticipation by prior public knowledge or use
4. Lack of novelty or inventive step
5. Non-disclosure of information (Section 8 obligation violated)
6. Non-patentable subject matter (Section 3/4)
7. Insufficient or unclear description
8. Failure to disclose best method of performing the invention
9. False claim of priority
10. Sec 39 violation (foreign filing permission)

**Section 25(2) — Post-Grant Opposition** (within 12 months of grant)
Same grounds as above, but can only be filed by any "person interested."

**Section 25(3) — Revocation** (before Appellate Board or High Court)
- Patent obtained by fraud
- Non-compliance with Section 8 (duty to disclose foreign applications)
- Non-working of the patent in India (Section 83)

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark per 2 distinct grounds, 1 mark for distinguishing pre-grant vs. post-grant.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the patentability of biotechnological inventions. With examples, explain the difference between the Indian and US approaches to biopatents.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** What is a compulsory license? Under what circumstances can the Controller General of Patents grant a compulsory license under Section 84?

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Patentability of Biotechnological Inventions**

Biotechnology inventions occupy a contested space in patent law, raising unique ethical, legal, and scientific questions. The Indian Patents Act, 1970 (post-2005 amendment) recognizes the patentability of:

- Recombinant DNA sequences with stated function
- Monoclonal antibodies and their production processes
- Genetically engineered microorganisms (e.g., oil-eating bacteria)
- Novel proteins and enzymes
- Transgenic plant cells (BUT not whole plants under Section 3(j))

**Indian Approach — Restrictive, Bioethics-Conscious:**

| Aspect | Indian Position | Statutory Basis |
|---|---|---|
| Plants/animals (essentially biological) | **Not patentable** | Section 3(j) |
| Biological processes (essentially biological) | **Not patentable** | Section 3(j) |
| Microorganisms | **Patentable** | Section 3(j) Proviso |
| Recombinant products | **Patentable** | No exclusion |
| Traditional knowledge | **Protected** (TKDL database) | Section 3(p) |
| Human genes (natural) | **Not patentable** | Section 3(c) |

**US Approach — Liberal, Discovery-Oriented:**

The United States, under the USPTO guidelines, has historically granted patents on:
- **Diamond v. Chakrabarty (1980)** — Genetically engineered bacterium patentable
- **First isolated BRCA1 gene patent (Myriad Genetics, 1996–2013)** — Isolated DNA patentable
- **OncoMouse (1988)** — Transgenic animal patentable
- **cDNA (complementary DNA)** — Patentable as it does not occur in nature

> **The 2013 Association for Molecular Pathology v. Myriad Genetics** judgment (US Supreme Court) marked a shift: **naturally occurring DNA segments are products of nature and not patentable**, but **cDNA is patentable**. This brought the US closer to the Indian position.

**Biopatents — Classic Indian Case Studies:**
- **Neem Patent** (European Patent Office, 2000): Revoked Indian traditional knowledge-based neem biopesticide patent granted to W.R. Grace.
- **Turmeric Patent** (USPTO, 1995): Revoked University of Mississippi patent on wound-healing properties of turmeric on the basis of prior Indian traditional knowledge.
- **Basmati Patent** (RiceTec, 1997): Largely defeated due to geographical indication protection.
- **Bt Cotton Patent** (Monsanto, 2002): Substantively granted in India, with controlled sub-licensing through MAHYCO.

*(Mark Distribution: 2 marks for the Indian framework, 2 marks for the US framework, 3 marks for examples, 1 mark for comparative conclusion.)*

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Definition of Compulsory License (Section 84):**

A **compulsory license** is a government-authorized permission to a third party to manufacture, sell, or import a patented invention **without the consent of the patent holder**, on prescribed terms including payment of royalty to the patentee.

**Grounds for Granting a Compulsory License (Section 84(1)):**

The Controller General may grant a compulsory license if ALL of the following conditions are satisfied:

1. **Reasonable time has elapsed** since the patent was granted — typically at least **3 years**.

2. **The patented invention is not worked in India** (i.e., not manufactured in India to an adequate extent, or not worked to the fullest extent reasonably practicable).

3. **The patented invention fails to satisfy reasonable public requirements** — i.e., not available at a reasonably affordable price, or refused to be made available.

4. **The patentee has not worked the invention in India by reason of importation from abroad.**

5. **The applicant must have made reasonable efforts to obtain a voluntary license from the patentee on reasonable commercial terms**, and such efforts have not succeeded within a reasonable period of time.

**Procedural Conditions (Section 84(6) and (7)):**

- The Controller must be satisfied that the **granting of the license is in public interest**.
- The license is **non-exclusive**.
- The licensee must work the invention in India predominantly for the Indian market.
- **Royalty or other remuneration** must be paid to the patentee as determined by the Controller.
- The license is subject to **review, modification, or revocation** on changed circumstances.

**Key Case Study:**

**Natco v. Bayer (Compulsory License No. 1 of 2012):**
In 2012, the Controller General issued India's first-ever compulsory license allowing Natco Pharma to manufacture the anti-cancer drug **Sorafenib Tosylate (Nexavar)** of Bayer, at a price ~97% lower than Bayer's market price. The grounds were:
- Bayer's failure to work the patent in India
- Unreasonably high pricing (Rs. 2.8 lakh/month vs. Natco's Rs. 8,800/month)
- Failure to make the drug available to the public in reasonable quantity

This judgment was upheld by multiple appellate authorities, including the Supreme Court, and remains a landmark precedent for public-health-driven IP policy.

*(Mark Distribution: 2 marks for definition and statutory basis, 3 marks for the 5 grounds, 2 marks for procedural conditions, 1 mark for Natco v. Bayer as illustration.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Patent Law Questions**
> 1. **Most common mark-loss pitfall #1:** Citing only "Section 3" generically instead of the specific sub-clause (e.g., "3(j)" for biological subject matter). Always quote the exact sub-section number.
> 2. **Most common mark-loss pitfall #2:** Writing "20 years from grant" instead of "20 years from FILING." The filing date is the operative trigger.
> 3. **Most common mark-loss pitfall #3:** Confusing **Compulsory License** (Section 84) with **Revocation** (Section 85/25). They are different remedies with different prerequisites.
> 4. **Most common mark-loss pitfall #4:** Missing the **non-working in India** requirement in compulsory license answers. This is a TRIPS Article 31 obligation and is mandatory.
> 5. **Most common mark-loss pitfall #5:** Writing "Copyright" or "Trademark" when the question asks for "Patent." Triple-check the question stem.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> **Pre-Exam Rapid-Fire Recap (Last-Minute Revision)**

- [ ] **Definition:** Patent = 20-year statutory monopoly in exchange for public disclosure.
- [ ] **Patent Term:** 20 years from **FILING DATE** (not grant date) — Section 53(1).
- [ ] **Three Pillars (NICE):** **N**ovelty, **I**nventive Step, Industrial Application, plus a fourth "E" — **E**xclusivity.
- [ ] **Statutory Anchor:** The Patents Act, 1970; amended in 1999, 2002, 2005.
- [ ] **Biopatents in India:** Allowed for microorganisms and recombinant products; NOT allowed for plants/animals or natural genes.
- [ ] **Diamond v. Chakrabarty (1980):** Foundation case for biopatents globally — engineered organism is patentable.
- [ ] **Myriad Genetics (2013):** Naturally occurring DNA is NOT patentable; cDNA IS patentable.
- [ ] **2005 Amendment:** India transitioned to a **product patent regime** in pharma/agro.
- [ ] **Publication:** Automatic at 18 months; can request early publication.
- [ ] **RFE:** Must be filed within 48 months; else application deemed abandoned.
- [ ] **Renewal:** Starts from 3rd year; missed renewal = patent lapses.
- [ ] **Pre-grant opposition (Sec 25(1)):** Any person, anytime from publication to grant.
- [ ] **Post-grant opposition (Sec 25(2)):** Person interested, within 12 months of grant.
- [ ] **Compulsory License (Sec 84):** Only after 3 years; only if patentee failed to work in India; only after unsuccessful voluntary negotiation.
- [ ] **Natco v. Bayer (2012):** First Indian compulsory license — Sorafenib Tosylate (Nexavar).
- [ ] **Paris Convention Priority:** 12 months for patent from first foreign filing.
