- [ ] **PCT National Phase:** 30 or 31 months from priority date, depending on jurisdiction.
- [ ] **Section 3(j):** Excludes plants/animals in whole; allows microorganisms by proviso.
- [ ] **Section 3(i):** Methods of treatment of humans/animals are not patentable.
- [ ] **TRIPS Compliance:** India amended its patent law in 2005 to comply with TRIPS Article 65 deadline.
- [ ] **Budapest Treaty:** Governs international deposit of microorganisms for patent purposes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Strategy Tip**
> In 14-mark questions, *always* conclude with a **bioethics connection** — e.g., the ethical implications of patenting human genes, the farmer's rights vs. Monsanto's Bt cotton patents, or the access-to-medicines debate. Examiners award 1–2 bonus marks for such contemporary and reflective insights. Frame the discussion in terms of *TRIPS flexibilities, Doha Declaration, and the right to health.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Authorship and Author Disputes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT522 - BIOETHICS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Authorship and Author Disputes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Authorship** in scholarly publishing refers to the formal attribution of intellectual and creative contribution to individuals who have made a substantive, defensible contribution to the conception, design, execution, analysis, or interpretation of a research study reported in a published manuscript. Under the **International Committee of Medical Journal Editors (ICMJE)** framework — the globally accepted standard adopted by the majority of KTU-referenced journals — authorship carries not merely credit but explicit **accountability, ethical responsibility, and legal liability** for the integrity of the published work.

An **Author Dispute** arises when there is conflict, disagreement, or ambiguity regarding **who qualifies as an author**, **the order in which authors are listed**, **the degree of contribution claimed**, **ghost authorship** (omission of legitimate contributors), or **guest/honorary authorship** (inclusion of non-contributors). Disputes may also involve disputes over **data ownership**, **intellectual contribution**, or **post-publication retraction demands**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Authorship is not a courtesy — it is a contractual ethical commitment that binds the author to public responsibility for the validity, originality, and ethical soundness of every claim in the manuscript.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a film production:

- The **Director** (Principal/Corresponding Author) is the one who conceived the project, made the creative decisions, and is held publicly accountable if the film contains defamatory or plagiarized content.
- The **Lead Actors** (First and Co-First Authors) executed the central creative performance and did most of the visible "shooting."
- The **Supporting Cast and Cinematographer** (Co-Authors) made indispensable technical contributions.
- The **Caterer who supplied food on set** (Acknowledged Contributor) is thanked in the credits but is **not** credited as an "author of the film."

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Lock-in:** Just as a film's credits list every person who actually shaped the final cut — and not the producer's nephew who merely visited the set — a research paper's author list must reflect *only* those who genuinely shaped the intellectual content. Putting a senior professor's name on a paper they never read (a **"guest authorship"**) is as misleading as listing a film's caterer as a co-director.

### 1.3 Why Authorship Matters in B.Tech / Biotechnology Research

For KTU B.Tech students — particularly those in Biotechnology, Food Technology, and Biomedical Engineering streams who will inevitably co-author technical papers, conference proceedings, and project reports — authorship is the **primary currency of academic credit**. It determines:

1. **Promotions and admissions** to higher studies (M.Tech, Ph.D., GATE rankings).
2. **Funding eligibility** for future research grants.
3. **Professional reputation** in industry and academia.
4. **Legal accountability** in cases of research misconduct.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** Module 1 of PEBBT522 explicitly grounds authorship within the broader framework of **research ethics**, making it a foundational pre-requisite for understanding the safety and ethics modules that follow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

### 2.1 The ICMJE Four-Criteria Authorship Test (The Global Gold Standard)

The **ICMJE Recommendations (2013, updated 2017, 2018, 2024)** specify that authorship should be based on **ALL four** of the following criteria — not just one or two. **All four must be met.**

| # | Criterion | Operational Meaning for B.Tech Researchers |
|---|-----------|---------------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Substantial contribution** to conception/design **OR** acquisition/analysis/interpretation of data | Did the person actually generate data, run experiments, or build the theoretical model? Merely providing a reagent or a software tool is **not** enough. |
| 2 | **Drafting the work** OR **critical revision for important intellectual content** | The person must have either written major sections of the manuscript **or** reviewed and intellectually challenged the drafts in a substantive way. |
| 3 | **Final approval** of the version to be published | The author must read and sign off on the *final* manuscript — not just an early draft. |
| 4 | **Accountability** for all aspects of the work | The author must be willing to publicly defend **every** part of the paper, including parts they did not personally generate. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All Four Criteria Are Conjunctive (AND, not OR).** A person who meets only Criterion 1 and 4 — but did not help draft or critically revise — does **NOT** qualify as an author. They may be acknowledged, but not listed.

### 2.2 Authorship-Related Concepts — The Definitional Matrix

| Term | Definition | Ethical Status |
|------|------------|----------------|
| **Authorship** | Listing as a named author on a manuscript | Earned through criteria |
| **Co-authorship** | Shared authorship with substantive contribution | Legitimate and expected |
| **First authorship** | The position of greatest individual credit in life sciences | Reflects primary contributor |
| **Corresponding author** | The person handling communication and accountability | Responsible for the work |
| **Senior/Last authorship** | The position of the supervising PI/lab head in many fields | Reflects mentorship |
| **Ghost authorship** | A person who wrote/contributed substantially but is **omitted** from the author list | **Research misconduct** |
| **Guest/Honorary/Gift authorship** | A person listed who did **not** meet authorship criteria | **Research misconduct** |
| **Coerced authorship** | Inclusion of a senior figure under pressure or convention without contribution | **Research misconduct** |
| **Acknowledged contributor** | Listed in "Acknowledgements" section only | Ethically correct for non-qualifying contributors |
| **CRediT (Contributor Roles Taxonomy)** | A standardized 14-role taxonomy for declaring specific contributions | Increasingly required by journals |

### 2.3 Common Causes of Author Disputes — Analytical Breakdown

#### A. Disputes About **Inclusion** (Who should be on the list?)
1. **Gift authorship** — A senior professor expects their name on every paper from their lab.
2. **Coerced authorship** — A collaborator demands authorship in exchange for providing samples/reagents.
3. **Ghost authorship** — A medical writer or industry statistician drafts the paper but is hidden.
4. **Equal-contribution disputes** — Two researchers each claim to have done the most work.

#### B. Disputes About **Order** (Who is listed where?)
1. **First-author rivalry** — Between two graduate students who worked in parallel.
2. **Co-first authorship negotiation** — The increasing use of "joint first authorship" and how it is denoted (e.g., asterisks, footnote).
3. **Last-author credit** — Confusion over whether the lab head or the senior postdoc should be last.

#### C. Disputes About **Withdrawal** (Post-submission retraction demands)
1. An author discovers fabricated data and demands retraction.
2. A contributor is **removed** from the author list during revision without consent.
3. A junior author publishes without senior author's consent, leading to dispute.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Research Utility

- **Biotechnology industry** — Authorship practices in industrial publications often involve **ghost authorship by medical communication agencies** writing for pharma companies. The **Gøtzsche and Kassirer (2010s)** controversies in medical literature highlight this.
- **Academic research labs** — The **Hwang Woo-suk (2005) cloning scandal** is a landmark case where authorship misattribution and data fabrication in *Science* journal led to retractions, criminal charges, and the end of the researcher's career.
- **Engineering and patent disputes** — Misattributed authorship in technical reports can lead to **patent invalidation** and IP litigation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters to KTU Students:** As a B.Tech student submitting a final-year project report or co-authoring a conference paper, understanding authorship criteria *before* you list names prevents career-damaging disputes later. The order you agree upon **now** determines your professional identity for decades.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Authorship Decision Tree & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Step-by-Step Authorship Eligibility Test (ICMJE-Aligned)

The following is the exhaustive decision procedure used by journal editors, ethics committees, and research integrity offices worldwide. Every step must be answered **affirmatively** for authorship to be granted.

#### **Step 1 — Identify the Candidate**

Start with every individual who *might* be on the author list. For a typical KTU B.Tech project, candidates may include:
- The student(s) who performed the work.
- The project guide / supervisor.
- Co-investigators from collaborating departments.
- Industry mentors (if part of an internship project).
- Technical staff who ran specific equipment.

#### **Step 2 — Apply Criterion 1: Substantive Contribution**

Ask, with documentary evidence (lab notebooks, commit logs, data files):

$$
\text{Did the person } (x) \text{ contribute to ANY of:} 
\begin{cases}
\text{Conception or design of the work} \\
\text{Data acquisition (experiments, surveys, code runs)} \\
\text{Data analysis or interpretation}
\end{cases}
$$

If **YES** to any one of these sub-clauses → Criterion 1 satisfied → proceed to Step 3.
If **NO** to all → The person does **NOT** qualify as an author → place in Acknowledgements.

#### **Step 3 — Apply Criterion 2: Manuscript Crafting**

$$
\text{Did the person } (x) \text{ either:}
\begin{cases}
\text{Draft the manuscript (initial writing)} \quad \text{OR} \\
\text{Critically revise it for important intellectual content}
\end{cases}
$$

The "critical revision" clause **is not** a copy-edit or typo-fix. It must be a substantive intellectual challenge that *changed* the paper. A reviewer who only fixed grammar does **not** qualify.

If **YES** → Criterion 2 satisfied → proceed to Step 4.
If **NO** → The person does **NOT** qualify as an author.

#### **Step 4 — Apply Criterion 3: Final Approval**

Did the person read and **explicitly approve** the final version of the manuscript (typically by signing an authorship form, an electronic license, or an email confirmation to the corresponding author)?

If **YES** → Criterion 3 satisfied → proceed to Step 5.
If **NO** → The person does **NOT** qualify. Adding a name "on their behalf" is **ghost or gift authorship**.

#### **Step 5 — Apply Criterion 4: Public Accountability**

Is the person prepared to publicly defend **every aspect** of the work — including parts they did not personally do? This is the most demanding criterion and the one most often overlooked.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Accountability Clause is Unique to ICMJE.** Even if a co-author personally did only the statistical analysis, they are publicly accountable for the **biology, ethics, and conclusions** of the entire paper. This is why authorship is not a casual courtesy.

#### **Step 6 — Determine Order (Position)**

Once the author list is finalized, the order is determined by:

$$
\text{Order Position} = f(\text{Individual Contribution Magnitude}, \text{Field Convention}, \text{Negotiated Agreement})
$$

In **biomedical and life sciences** (relevant to PEBBT522):

$$
\text{First Author} = \text{primary contributor (often the student who did the work)}
$$

$$
\text{Middle Authors} = \text{other substantive contributors, often in descending contribution order}
$$

$$
\text{Last Author} = \text{Senior Author / Principal Investigator (the lab head)}
$$

$$
\text{Corresponding Author} = \text{whoever handles communication, often the PI}
$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Case Walkthrough — The B.Tech Project Scenario

**Scenario:** A KTU B.Tech Biotechnology final-year group of three students (A, B, C) works under Professor P. Student A did the experiments, Student B did the bioinformatics analysis, Student C did statistical analysis and helped draft the introduction. Professor P conceived the project, secured funding, and provided critical intellectual revisions.

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

| Person | C1 (Contribution) | C2 (Drafting/Revision) | C3 (Final Approval) | C4 (Accountability) | Verdict |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|---------|
| **Student A** | YES — ran all wet-lab experiments | YES — drafted Results section | YES — signed off | YES — willing to defend | **AUTHOR** |
| **Student B** | YES — did all bioinformatics | YES — drafted Methods (bioinformatics) | YES — signed off | YES — willing to defend | **AUTHOR** |
| **Student C** | YES — statistical analysis + intro draft | YES — drafted Introduction | YES — signed off | YES — willing to defend | **AUTHOR** |
| **Professor P** | YES — conceived project, funded it | YES — provided critical intellectual revisions | YES — signed off | YES — willing to defend | **AUTHOR** |
| **Lab Technician T** | YES — prepared reagents, sterilized glassware | NO — did not draft or revise | (N/A) | (N/A) | **NOT AN AUTHOR** → Acknowledged |

**Authorship order decision (justified):**

$$
\text{First Author} = \text{Student A (largest single experimental contribution)}
$$

$$
\text{Second Author} = \text{Student B (parallel substantial contribution)}
$$

$$
\text{Third Author} = \text{Student C (smaller but qualifying contribution)}
$$

$$
\text{Last/Corresponding Author} = \text{Professor P (PI, conceived and funded the work)}
$$

### 3.3 Dispute Resolution Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

When a dispute arises, the following sequential algorithm — modeled on **COPE (Committee on Publication Ethics)** flowcharts — must be applied:

$$
\text{Dispute} \rightarrow \text{Step A: Internal Resolution} \rightarrow \text{Step B: Institutional Mediation} \rightarrow \text{Step C: Editorial/Committee Adjudication} \rightarrow \text{Step D: Formal Misconduct Investigation}
$$

**Step A — Internal Resolution (mandatory first):**
1. The corresponding author convenes a meeting of all co-authors.
2. Each person's contribution is documented against the **CRediT taxonomy** (14 roles).
3. A consensus is reached on order, inclusion, and acknowledgement.
4. A signed **Authorship Agreement Form** is filed.

**Step B — Institutional Mediation:**
If internal resolution fails, the matter is escalated to:
- The Departmental Research Committee,
- The Institutional Ethics Committee (IEC),
- Or the Dean of Research.

**Step C — Editorial Adjudication:**
For manuscripts already submitted, the journal editor may:
- Request an authorship change,
- Reject the manuscript,
- Or hold it pending institutional resolution.

**Step D — Formal Misconduct Investigation:**
For proven misconduct (ghost/gift authorship, fabrication), the institution may impose sanctions including **retraction, suspension, and notification to funding agencies and regulatory bodies**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prevention is better than cure.** The best dispute resolution happens **before submission**, with a written authorship agreement signed by every contributor at the start of the project.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Authorship Eligibility Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Candidate Person Identified] --> B{Did they make a substantive contribution to conception, design, data, or analysis?}
    B -- No --> Z1[Place in Acknowledgements Section]
    B -- Yes --> C{Did they help draft the manuscript OR provide critical intellectual revision?}
    C -- No --> Z1
    C -- Yes --> D{Did they approve the FINAL version of the manuscript?}
    D -- No --> Z2[Do NOT list as author. This is ghost or gift authorship.]
    D -- Yes --> E{Are they willing to be publicly accountable for ALL aspects of the work?}
    E -- No --> Z2
    E -- Yes --> F[QUALIFIES AS AUTHOR]
    F --> G{Determine Order}
    G --> H[First Author: Largest Individual Contribution]
    G --> I[Middle Authors: Descending Contribution]
    G --> J[Last/Corresponding: Senior PI or Supervisor]
```

### 4.2 Author Dispute Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Authorship Dispute Arises] --> Q{Where in the publication lifecycle?}
    Q -- Pre-submission --> R[Step 1: Convene Co-author Meeting]
    R --> S[Step 2: Document Contributions via CRediT Taxonomy]
    S --> T[Step 3: Negotiate Order and Inclusion]
    T --> U[Step 4: Sign Written Authorship Agreement]
    Q -- Post-submission --> V[Step 5: Notify Journal Editor Immediately]
    V --> W[Step 6: Escalate to Institutional Ethics Committee]
    W --> X[Step 7: Formal Investigation if Misconduct Proven]
    X --> Y[Possible Outcomes: Retraction, Sanction, Legal Action]
    U --> OK[Dispute Resolved Ethically]
    Y --> OK
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Roles in a Research Publication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IdeationLayer["IDEATION LAYER"]
        PI[Principal Investigator / Lab Head]:::rolePI
        CO[Co-Investigator / Collaborator]:::roleCO
    end
    subgraph ExecutionLayer["EXECUTION LAYER"]
        SA[First Author / PhD Scholar / B.Tech Project Student]:::roleExec
        RA[Research Associate / Co-Author]:::roleExec
    end
    subgraph SupportLayer["SUPPORT LAYER"]
        TECH[Technician / Statistician]:::roleSupport
        WRITER[Medical Writer / Editor]:::roleSupport
    end
    subgraph PublishingLayer["PUBLISHING LAYER"]
        CA[Corresponding Author]:::rolePub
        JOUR[Journal Editor and Reviewers]:::rolePub
    end
    PI --> SA
    PI --> RA
    CO --> SA
    SA --> TECH
    SA --> WRITER
    SA --> CA
    CA --> JOUR
    classDef rolePI fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef roleCO fill:#2e5d8a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef roleExec fill:#3d7bb8,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef roleSupport fill:#c9a227,stroke:#000000,color:#000000
    classDef rolePub fill:#7a1f1f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation of the Block Diagram:**

- The **Ideation Layer** holds the **accountability weight** (highest).
- The **Execution Layer** holds the **credit weight** (highest credit to first author).
- The **Support Layer** may legitimately contribute but is acknowledged, not authored — *unless* they meet all four ICMJE criteria.
- The **Publishing Layer** is the **gatekeeper** that enforces ICMJE compliance post-submission.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Notice that the Technician and Writer sit in the Support Layer, not the Execution Layer. This visual separation is the clearest way to internalize *why* they receive acknowledgements rather than authorship. Move them up to Execution only if they satisfy all four criteria.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**"Define authorship. List the four ICMJE criteria for authorship."**
*(3 Marks — Blooms Level: Remember / Understand)*

**Model Answer (3 marks, expected structure):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Authorship in scholarly publishing is the formal attribution of intellectual contribution to individuals who have made a substantive, defensible contribution to a research study, along with the acceptance of public accountability for the work.

**Four ICMJE Criteria (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Substantial contribution** to the conception or design of the work; or the acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data.
2. **Drafting the work** or **critical revision** for important intellectual content.
3. **Final approval** of the version to be published.
4. **Accountability** for all aspects of the work, including the integrity and accuracy of the entire study.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students frequently list only the first two criteria and forget the **accountability clause** (Criterion 4). This is the most-tested distinction in KTU valuations. The examiner awards the final 0.5 mark only if Criterion 4 is mentioned.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**"Differentiate between ghost authorship and guest (honorary) authorship with examples."**
*(3 Marks — Blooms Level: Understand / Apply)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Ghost Authorship | Guest / Honorary Authorship |
|---------|------------------|------------------------------|
| **Definition** | A person who made a substantial contribution is **omitted** from the author list. | A person who made **no qualifying contribution** is **included** in the author list. |
| **Direction of error** | Concealment of a real contributor. | Falsely claiming credit by a non-contributor. |
| **Example** | A medical writer drafts a pharma-sponsored clinical trial paper but is not listed; only the named academic authors appear. | A senior department head is added to a student's project report simply because they are the head, with no intellectual contribution. |
| **Ethical status** | Research misconduct under COPE and ICMJE. | Research misconduct under COPE and ICMJE. |

(1 mark for the clear definition of each; 1 mark for the contrast/example.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often describe both as "fraud" without distinguishing the *direction* of the error. Examiners specifically test this contrast. Use the table format above for full marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) "Explain the ICMJE authorship guidelines in detail. Why is the accountability clause considered the most demanding criterion?" (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (i) — ICMJE Authorship Guidelines (4 marks):**

The International Committee of Medical Journal Editors (ICMJE) has established a globally accepted framework for determining who qualifies as an author of a scholarly publication. The guidelines require that **all four** of the following criteria be met simultaneously:

1. **Substantial Contribution:** The individual must have contributed significantly to either the conception or design of the study, the acquisition of data, the analysis of data, or the interpretation of findings. Routine technical support (e.g., preparing buffers) does not qualify.

2. **Manuscript Crafting or Critical Revision:** The individual must have either drafted the manuscript or provided substantive intellectual revisions that meaningfully improved the content. Mere language editing or formatting assistance does not qualify.

3. **Final Approval:** The individual must have reviewed and approved the final version of the manuscript that is being submitted for publication. This is typically documented through signed authorship forms or electronic licenses.

4. **Accountability:** The individual must accept public responsibility for all aspects of the work, including those parts they did not personally perform. This includes the integrity of the data, the validity of the conclusions, and the ethical soundness of the methodology.

**Part (ii) — Why the Accountability Clause is the Most Demanding (3 marks):**

- It imposes **public liability** — the author must defend the paper against criticism, even for sections they did not write.
- It exposes the author to **legal, reputational, and professional consequences** if misconduct (fabrication, falsification, plagiarism) is later discovered.
- It creates a **collective responsibility** that cannot be delegated — listing a name does not transfer accountability, it *adds* it.
- It effectively transforms authorship from a "credit" transaction into a "covenant" — a binding ethical commitment that cannot be revoked once the paper is published.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students describe the criteria as a checklist without explaining *why* accountability is qualitatively different. Examiners award the 3 marks for **synthesis and reasoning**, not mere recall.

---

**(b) "Discuss the common causes of author disputes in research. How can such disputes be prevented and resolved ethically? (7 Marks)"**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (i) — Common Causes (3 marks):**

1. **Inclusion Disputes:** Gift authorship (adding a senior figure), coerced authorship (demanding credit in exchange for resources), and ghost authorship (omitting a real contributor).
2. **Order Disputes:** Rivalry over first-authorship position, ambiguity in joint-first authorship, and disagreements over last-authorship credit for the PI.
3. **Post-Submission Disputes:** Disagreements during revision, demands for removal of a co-author, or unilateral submission without consensus.
4. **Data Ownership Disputes:** Conflict over who owns the raw data and whether they can be used in derivative publications.

**Part (ii) — Prevention Strategies (2 marks):**

1. Sign a **written authorship agreement** at the start of the project, with documented roles per the CRediT taxonomy.
2. Hold regular **co-author meetings** to review progress and contributions.
3. Agree in advance on the **order of authors** and the **corresponding author designation**.
4. Use institutional **Authorship Policy documents** (most KTU-affiliated institutions have these).

**Part (iii) — Resolution Pathway (2 marks):**

1. Internal discussion → 2. Departmental Research Committee → 3. Institutional Ethics Committee → 4. Journal Editor (if submitted) → 5. Formal misconduct investigation (if proven).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) "What is the CRediT taxonomy? Explain its 14 contributor roles and its importance in preventing authorship disputes." (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The CRediT (Contributor Roles Taxonomy) is a standardized classification system developed to describe the specific contributions of each author in a research publication. It was developed to bring transparency to authorship and reduce disputes over who did what.

**The 14 Roles (4 marks — 0.25 each for naming, 0.5 for explaining any 4 in detail):**

1. **Conceptualization** — Formulating the research idea or aim.
2. **Data curation** — Managing and maintaining research data.
3. **Formal analysis** — Applying statistical or mathematical techniques.
4. **Funding acquisition** — Securing financial support.
5. **Investigation** — Conducting the research and data collection.
6. **Methodology** — Designing the methods and models.
7. **Project administration** — Managing and coordinating the project.
8. **Resources** — Providing materials, reagents, or tools.
9. **Software** — Developing or implementing software code.
10. **Supervision** — Providing oversight and leadership.
11. **Validation** — Verifying results and reproducibility.
12. **Visualization** — Creating figures and tables.
13. **Writing – original draft** — Drafting the manuscript.
14. **Writing – review & editing** — Critical revision and editing.

**Importance in Preventing Disputes (2 marks):**

- Makes contributions **transparent and verifiable** — no ambiguity.
- Allows journals to **enforce accountability** by linking each role to a specific author.
- Prevents **gift authorship** by clarifying who actually did each task.
- Prevents **ghost authorship** by exposing hidden writers.
- Provides a **dispute-resolution reference document** that can be presented if conflicts arise post-submission.

---

**(b) "Analyze the Hwang Woo-suk scandal as a case study in authorship and research misconduct. What lessons does it offer to current B.Tech researchers? (7 Marks)"**

**Model Answer:**

**Background (1.5 marks):**
Hwang Woo-suk, a South Korean stem-cell researcher, published two landmark papers in *Science* (2004 and 2005) claiming to have created patient-matched embryonic stem cells. He was listed as the senior/corresponding author on both papers, with multiple co-authors from his lab at Seoul National University.

**Authorship-Related Misconducts Identified (3 marks):**

1. **Data fabrication** — The stem cell lines reported in the 2005 paper did not exist; photographs and data were fabricated.
2. **Coerced and gift authorship** — Several co-authors later testified that they had not seen the primary data and had been pressured to lend their names to the papers.
3. **Ghost contributors** — Junior researchers who actually did the work were allegedly pressured to stay silent.
4. **Failure of accountability** — As corresponding author, Hwang had signed the final approval, making him primarily responsible.

**Outcomes (1 mark):**
- Both papers were **retracted** from *Science*.
- Hwang was **dismissed** from Seoul National University.
- Several co-authors faced **legal and professional consequences**.
- South Korea passed stricter **Bioethics and Biosafety Act** amendments in 2005.

**Lessons for B.Tech Researchers (1.5 marks):**

1. Never add a name to a paper you have not read and verified.
2. Insist on **documented contributions** from the start of every project.
3. Whistleblowers within the lab (in this case, junior researchers) play a vital role — KTU institutions must protect them.
4. The **accountability clause** is not theoretical — it can lead to criminal liability.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often narrate the Hwang case as a generic "fraud story" without isolating the **authorship-specific** failures. Examiners award full marks only when the answer explicitly links misconduct to **authorship criteria violations** (failure of C2, C3, C4 of ICMJE).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Authorship ≠ Acknowledgement.** Authors are accountable; acknowledged contributors are thanked but not held liable.
- **The four ICMJE criteria are conjunctive (AND).** All four must be met.
- **The accountability clause (Criterion 4) is the most distinctive feature of ICMJE** and is heavily tested.
- **Ghost authorship = hiding a real contributor.** **Guest authorship = adding a fake contributor.** Both are misconduct.
- **First author** = primary contributor (often the student/researcher). **Last author** = supervising PI. **Corresponding author** = communication and accountability lead.
- **CRediT taxonomy** has 14 roles and is the modern tool for preventing disputes.
- **Dispute resolution order:** Internal meeting → Department → IEC → Journal Editor → Formal misconduct investigation.
- **Prevention is contractual:** A written authorship agreement signed at project start is the single most effective protection against disputes.
- **The Hwang Woo-suk (2005) case** is the most cited real-world case linking authorship failure to scientific misconduct.
- **For B.Tech students:** The names you put on your project report and papers are the foundation of your academic identity — protect them by writing *only* the names that genuinely belong there.
- **One-line exam mnemonic:** *"Contribute, Craft, Confirm, Commit"* — the four Cs of ICMJE authorship.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biohazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT522 - BIOETHICS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Biohazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biohazard (Biological Hazard):** Any biological agent or biological condition that has the potential to cause harm to humans, animals, plants, or the environment. The term encompasses pathogenic microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites), biological toxins, prions, genetically modified organisms (GMOs), cell cultures, and any biologically derived material that poses a threat to public health, agricultural productivity, or ecological stability.

According to the **World Health Organization (WHO) Laboratory Biosafety Manual (4th Edition)** and aligned with the **National Institute of Health (NIH) Guidelines for Research Involving Recombinant or Synthetic Nucleic Acid Molecules**, a biohazard is formally categorized as a **"Risk Group"** organism based on its pathogenicity, mode of transmission, host range, and availability of preventive or therapeutic interventions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** Students must understand (i) the classification of biohazards, (ii) Biosafety Levels (BSL 1–4), (iii) routes of exposure, (iv) biohazard symbols and labeling protocols, and (v) principles of containment and decontamination.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Enemy" Framework

Imagine your body is a **fortress** 🏰, and your immune system is the **army stationed inside**. A **biohazard** is like a **saboteur or enemy combatant** trying to infiltrate the fortress. Some saboteurs are relatively harmless (a toddler with a water pistol 💧 — this is a **Risk Group 1** agent), while others are elite commandos with biological weapons (a fully armed special forces operative 🪖 — this is a **Risk Group 4** agent).

To protect the fortress, you install:
- **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** → The body armor of each soldier 🛡️
- **Engineering Controls** → Watchtowers, locked gates, blast doors 🏗️
- **Administrative Controls** → Curfews, training drills, ID checks 📋
- **Decontamination** → Decontamination showers after every mission 🚿

This analogy maps directly to the **hierarchy of controls** used in every microbiology lab, hospital, and biotech facility worldwide.

---

## 1.3 Categories of Biohazardous Agents (Master Overview)

| # | Category | Examples | Typical Risk |
|---|----------|----------|--------------|
| 1 | **Bacteria** | *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, *Salmonella typhi* | Moderate–High |
| 2 | **Viruses** | HIV, H1N1, SARS-CoV-2, Ebola | Low–Extreme |
| 3 | **Fungi** | *Histoplasma capsulatum*, *Candida auris* | Moderate |
| 4 | **Parasites** | *Plasmodium falciparum*, *Trypanosoma cruzi* | Moderate–High |
| 5 | **Prions** | Creutzfeldt-Jakob Agent (PrP⁽ˢᶜ⁾) | Extreme |
| 6 | **Biological Toxins** | Botulinum toxin, Ricin, Staphylococcal enterotoxin | High |
| 7 | **Genetically Modified Organisms (GMOs/Transgenics)** | Transgenic mice, r-DNA bacteria, gene therapy vectors | Variable |
| 8 | **Human/Animal Cell Cultures & Tissues** | HeLa cells, primary tissue biopsies, blood | Variable (often BSL-2) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used in Biohazard Assessment:**
> - **Infectious Dose (ID₅₀):** Number of organisms required to infect 50% of exposed hosts (can be as low as **1–10 organisms** for *E. coli* O157:H7 or **1–100 viral particles** for smallpox).
> - **LD₅₀ (Lethal Dose 50%):** Dose at which 50% of test animals die — measured in **mg/kg body weight**.
> - **Particle Size for Aerosol Biohazards:** Concern threshold is **≤ 5 μm** (respirable fraction that reaches alveoli).

---

## 1.4 The Universal Biohazard Symbol — "The Three-Crescent Trefoil"

The internationally recognized **biohazard symbol** was designed in **1966 by Charles Baldwin** at Dow Chemical Company. It is mandated under:
- **OSHA Standard 29 CFR 1910.1030** (U.S. Occupational Safety and Health Administration)
- **ISO 7010 — W009** (International Organization for Standardization)

> [!NOTE]
> The trefoil's design is **uniquely identifiable** even to a person who has never seen it before. Studies have shown it is recognizable across **language and literacy barriers** — a key reason it was adopted globally as the universal biohazard pictogram.

The symbol consists of **three interlocking crescents** arranged at **120° rotational symmetry**, representing the **trinity of biohazard transmission routes**: **Ingestion, Inoculation, and Absorption/Inhalation**.

---

## 1.5 Routes of Exposure — The "Five Ports of Entry"

Biohazardous agents gain entry into the host via **five primary routes**:

1. **Inhalation** (respiratory tract — most common in labs; aerosols < 5 μm)
2. **Ingestion** (hand-to-mouth transfer; eating in lab)
3. **Percutaneous** (needlesticks, cuts, broken skin)
4. **Mucous Membrane Contact** (eyes, nose, mouth)
5. **Transplacental / Transdermal** (mother to fetus; rare but catastrophic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to list routes of exposure, students often forget **transplacental transmission** (e.g., Zika virus, Rubella, CMV). Always list **5 routes**, not 3 or 4, to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The WHO/NIH Risk Group Classification System

The cornerstone of biohazard classification is the **4-Tier Risk Group (RG) System** adopted globally by WHO, NIH, CDC, and most national regulatory bodies. It is the *theoretical foundation* upon which the *practical* Biosafety Level (BSL) system is built.

| Risk Group | Pathogenicity | Transmission | Prophylaxis/Treatment | Examples |
|:----------:|---------------|--------------|----------------------|----------|
| **RG-1** | Low; unlikely to cause disease in healthy adults | N/A | None required | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, *Bacillus subtilis*, *E. coli* K-12 |
| **RG-2** | Moderate; can cause disease, but rarely serious; preventive/treatment available | Moderate | Available | *Salmonella*, *Staphylococcus aureus*, Herpes Simplex Virus, Influenza A |
| **RG-3** | High; causes serious/lethal disease; treatment usually available | High | Available (limited) | *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, SARS-CoV-2, *Brucella abortus*, West Nile Virus |
| **RG-4** | Extreme; causes severe, often untreatable disease; easily transmitted | Very High | **NO** prophylaxis or treatment | Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, Smallpox, Crimean-Congo Hemorrhagic Fever |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** Risk Group classification is based on the **agent's intrinsic properties**, not the **containment practices**. **Biosafety Level (BSL)** is based on the **containment practices required** to handle that agent. These are related but **NOT identical** concepts.

---

## 2.2 Biosafety Levels (BSL) — The Practical Implementation

The **CDC/NIH Biosafety in Microbiological and Biomedical Laboratories (BMBL) Manual** defines **four ascending Biosafety Levels (BSL-1 to BSL-4)**, each with progressively stricter engineering controls, PPE, and facility requirements.

### 2.2.1 BSL-1 — "The Basic Teaching Lab"
- **Agents:** RG-1 (non-pathogenic)
- **PPE:** Lab coat, gloves, safety glasses
- **Facility:** Open bench work permitted; sink with running water
- **Special Equipment:** None required
- **Examples of Use:** Teaching undergraduate microbiology, food microbiology quality control

### 2.2.2 BSL-2 — "The Clinical/Diagnostic Lab"
- **Agents:** RG-2 (moderate risk)
- **PPE:** Lab coat, double gloves, goggles, face shield (if splash risk)
- **Facility:** Biosafety Cabinet (BSC) Class II for aerosol-generating work; self-closing door
- **Special Equipment:** Class II BSC, autoclave available
- **Examples of Use:** Hospital diagnostic labs, clinical virology, most bloodborne pathogen work (HBV, HIV)

### 2.2.3 BSL-3 — "The High-Containment Lab" 🟠
- **Agents:** RG-3 (indigenous/exotic, aerosol-transmitted, lethal)
- **PPE:** Solid-front protective suit, PAPR (Powered Air-Purifying Respirator) or HEPA-filtered respirator (N95/FFP3)
- **Facility:** Negative pressure anteroom; HEPA-filtered exhaust air; sealed penetrations
- **Special Equipment:** Class II or III BSC, sealed centrifuge rotors, in-line HEPA filtration
- **Examples of Use:** TB labs, SARS-CoV-2 propagation, *Brucella*, *Francisella tularensis*

### 2.2.4 BSL-4 — "The Maximum Containment Lab" 🔴
- **Agents:** RG-4 (fatal, no treatment, aerosol-transmitted)
- **PPE:** Full-body positive-pressure suit ("space suit") OR Class III BSC with airtight cabinets
- **Facility:** Positive-pressure suit room OR Class III cabinet line with chemical shower; effluent decontamination
- **Special Equipment:** HEPA on supply AND exhaust, double-door autoclave, dedicated non-recirculating air handling
- **Examples of Use:** Ebola, Marburg, Lassa, smallpox research
- **Worldwide:** Only **~60 facilities** exist (USAMRIID, CDC, RML, Wuhan Institute, etc.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Conflating "Risk Group" with "Biosafety Level". A **Risk Group 3 agent may sometimes be handled at BSL-2** with enhanced precautions (e.g., clinical diagnostic specimens of *M. tuberculosis* before confirmation). The level of containment is **risk-assessed**, not always strictly tied to the RG number.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Classification Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike Physics or Mathematics, "formulas" in Bioethics are **classification matrices, decision algorithms, and risk-assessment equations**. The following table is your "cheat sheet" for any KTU biohazard question.

| Parameter | Definition | Standard / Unit |
|-----------|------------|-----------------|
| **ID₅₀** | Infectious dose for 50% of hosts | Organisms / host |
| **LD₅₀** | Lethal dose for 50% of test animals | mg/kg body weight |
| **TCID₅₀** | Tissue Culture Infectious Dose (50%) | TCID₅₀/mL |
| **PFU** | Plaque Forming Unit (viral titre) | PFU/mL |
| **CFU** | Colony Forming Unit (bacterial titre) | CFU/mL |
| **HEPA filter efficiency** | Captures ≥ **99.97%** of particles ≥ **0.3 μm** | % retention |
| **Autoclave cycle (biohazard waste)** | 121°C, 15 psi, ≥ **30 minutes** (gravity) / ≥ **20 minutes** (prevacuum) | Time / Temperature / Pressure |
| **BSC Class I** | Personnel + environment protection; no product | Air velocity: 75 lf/min |
| **BSC Class II** | Personnel + environment + product | Type A2 most common |
| **BSC Class III** | Gas-tight; total containment | Used in BSL-4 |
| **Hand hygiene** | 20 seconds with WHO 6-step method | Duration |
| **Spill response radius (RG-2)** | **30 cm** containment | Distance |
| **Spill response radius (RG-3)** | **1–3 m** containment | Distance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility — The "Hierarchy of Controls" in Real Facilities:** This is the foundational occupational safety model adopted by NIOSH, OSHA, and the WHO. **Most effective** at the top, **least effective** at the bottom:
>
> **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE**
>
> PPE is the **LAST** line of defense, not the first. Many KTU questions test this hierarchy.

---

## 2.4 Biohazard Waste Management Decision Tree (Theory)

> [!NOTE]
> Biohazardous waste is regulated under the **BMW (Biomedical Waste Management) Rules, 2016 (and 2018 amendments)** in India. Misclassification carries **₹1 lakh–₹25 lakh fines** and **5-year imprisonment** under the **Environment Protection Act, 1986**.

| Category | Waste Type | Treatment | Disposal |
|:--------:|------------|-----------|----------|
| **Yellow** | Anatomical, soiled, chemical | Incineration / deep burial | Ash to TSDF |
| **Red** | Contaminated recyclable plastic (tubing, bottles) | Autoclaving / chemical disinfection | Recycling |
| **White (Sharp containers)** | Needles, scalpels, blades | Autoclaving / dry heat | Shredding / encapsulation |
| **Blue** | Glassware, broken glass | Disinfection + recycling | Glass recycling |

**Color-coding is mandatory** in India under Schedule-I of the BMW Rules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS, ANALYTICAL MATRIX & PROCEDURAL REASONING

> [!NOTE]
> Bioethics is procedural and regulatory. The "derivations" in this module are **decision trees, standard operating procedures (SOPs), and risk-assessment algorithms** rather than algebraic equations. Below, every step is written out exhaustively to give you a model answer for any KTU descriptive question.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The Risk Assessment Algorithm

A biohazard risk assessment follows a **standardized 6-step logic chain** recognized by the WHO Biorisk Management framework. The procedure is **deductive** — each step refines the output of the previous step.

### **STEP 1 — Identify the Agent**
Determine:
- Taxonomic identity (genus, species, strain)
- Is it a known human pathogen?
- Is it wild-type, attenuated, or genetically modified?

$$\text{Agent Identity} = \{ \text{Organism}, \text{Strain}, \text{Modification Status} \}$$

If the agent is a **known human pathogen**, proceed to Step 2. If it is **non-pathogenic** (e.g., *E. coli* K-12, *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*), classify as **RG-1 / BSL-1** and STOP the algorithm.

### **STEP 2 — Assign the Risk Group**
Consult the **NIH Risk Group Database**, **ABSA International database**, or **WHO Laboratory Biosafety Manual** to assign the Risk Group (RG-1 to RG-4).

$$\text{Risk Group} = f(\text{Pathogenicity}, \text{Transmission}, \text{Host Range}, \text{Treatment Availability})$$

Where $f$ is the regulatory classification function defined in **Appendix B of the NIH Guidelines (2019 revision)**.

### **STEP 3 — Determine the Biosafety Level**
The BSL is **typically equal to the Risk Group** in most routine cases, but may be **modified up or down** based on:

$$\text{BSL}_{\text{required}} = \text{RG} + \Delta_{\text{activity}} + \Delta_{\text{volume}} + \Delta_{\text{aerosol}} + \Delta_{\text{host}}$$

Where:
- $\Delta_{\text{activity}} = +1$ if concentrated virus production (vs. diagnostic work)
- $\Delta_{\text{volume}} = +1$ if working with >10 L culture
- $\Delta_{\text{aerosol}} = +1$ if aerosol-generating procedure (sonication, vortexing, blender)
- $\Delta_{\text{host}} = +1$ if host is immunocompromised (e.g., SCID mouse)

**Example derivation:** Working with **West Nile Virus (RG-3)** in a tissue culture flask with sonication steps:
$$\text{BSL}_{\text{required}} = 3 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 = 4$$

→ BSL-3 + aerosol precautions, but if **propagated in large volumes**, BSL-3 with enhanced respiratory protection is required.

### **STEP 4 — Apply the Hierarchy of Controls**
In strict order of preference:

1. **Elimination** — Can the experiment be avoided? → If yes, do so.
2. **Substitution** — Can a non-pathogenic surrogate be used? (e.g., *Bacillus subtilis* instead of *B. anthracis*)
3. **Engineering Controls** — BSC, sealed rotors, HEPA filtration
4. **Administrative Controls** — SOPs, training, signage, restricted access
5. **PPE** — Gloves, lab coat, respirator, face shield

### **STEP 5 — Validate Decontamination**
- **Autoclave validation:** Use **biological indicator (Geobacillus stearothermophilus spore strip)** weekly.
- **Acceptable kill:** **≥ 10⁶ log reduction** (i.e., 99.9999% kill)
- **Disinfectant validation:** Use appropriate concentration × contact time:
  - **1:10 sodium hypochlorite** (10,000 ppm) for general biohazard spill
  - **70% ethanol** for surface decontamination (less effective against spores)
  - **Quaternary ammonium compounds** for routine cleaning (not sporicidal)
  - **2% glutaraldehyde** for instruments (high-level disinfection)

### **STEP 6 — Document, Audit, and Review**
- Maintain **biosafety manual**, **incident log**, **training records**, **medical surveillance records**
- Review **annually** or upon protocol change (per **ISO 35001:2019 — Biorisk management for laboratories**)

---

## 3.2 Procedural Derivation: Biohazard Spill Response (RG-2 Liquid Spill)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU. Memorize the 8-step protocol below.

**STEP 1: Stop work, evacuate the immediate area** (typically 30 cm radius for RG-2)

**STEP 2: Allow aerosols to settle** (minimum **30 minutes** for RG-2; **60 minutes** for RG-3)

**STEP 3: Don appropriate PPE**
- Lab coat, double gloves, N95 respirator (if aerosol risk), goggles

**STEP 4: Contain the spill**
- Cover with absorbent paper towels
- Pour disinfectant **from the periphery inward** (to contain the spill)
- Use **1:10 bleach** (final concentration 10,000 ppm available chlorine)

**STEP 5: Allow contact time**
- **≥ 20 minutes** for bleach against most vegetative bacteria
- **≥ 30 minutes** for viruses and mycobacteria

**STEP 6: Collect waste**
- Place all contaminated materials in **red biohazard bag**
- Autoclave before final disposal

**STEP 7: Decontaminate the area**
- Clean with disinfectant, then with water
- Dispose of PPE as biohazard waste

**STEP 8: Report the incident**
- Notify the **Institutional Biosafety Committee (IBSC)** and **Biosafety Officer (BSO)**
- Document in incident register
- Initiate **medical surveillance** if exposure suspected

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Decontamination Methods

| Method | Mechanism | Effectiveness vs. Spores | Time | Use Case |
|--------|-----------|--------------------------|------|----------|
| **Autoclaving** | Steam + pressure denatures protein | ✅ **Effective** (with proper cycle) | 30–60 min | Liquid culture, solid waste |
| **Dry Heat (160°C)** | Oxidation | ✅ Effective | 2 hr | Glassware, oils |
| **Incineration** | Combustion | ✅ Effective (definitive) | Continuous | Anatomical waste, sharps |
| **Bleach (1:10)** | Oxidation of proteins/nucleic acids | ❌ Ineffective on spores at low conc. | 20–30 min | Surface decon, spill |
| **Ethanol (70%)** | Protein coagulation | ❌ Ineffective on spores | 1–5 min | Skin, work surfaces |
| **Glutaraldehyde (2%)** | Alkylation of proteins | ⚠ Sporicidal only after **10 hours** | Hours | Endoscopes, instruments |
| **UV-C (254 nm)** | Pyrimidine dimer formation | ❌ Limited | Variable | Surface, BSC interior |
| **Virkon® (peroxygen)** | Oxidation | ✅ Broad-spectrum | 10 min | Footbaths, surface |
| **Plasma Sterilization** | Free radicals (H₂O₂) | ✅ Effective | 1 hr | Heat-sensitive instruments |

---

## 3.4 Decision Logic: Which PPE for Which BSL?

| BSL | Body | Hands | Eyes | Respiratory |
|:---:|------|-------|------|-------------|
| **BSL-1** | Lab coat | Nitrile gloves | Safety glasses | None (bench work) |
| **BSL-2** | Lab coat + gown | Double gloves | Goggles + face shield (if splash) | Surgical mask (if risk) |
| **BSL-3** | Solid-front suit | Double gloves | Goggles | PAPR or N100/FFP3 |
| **BSL-4** | Positive-pressure suit (orange "space suit") | Triple gloves | Goggles | Supplied-air respirator (SCBA) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are rendered as **Mermaid block diagrams** (KTU-preferred, board-presentation style). All node IDs are alphanumeric and all labels are clean plain text per protocol.

---

## 4.1 Biohazard Classification Master Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    startAgent[Identify Biological Agent] --> isPath{Is it a Human or Animal Pathogen?}
    isPath -- No --> rg1[Risk Group 1 - BSL 1 - No containment needed]
    isPath -- Yes --> rgClass{What is the Severity and Transmission Risk?}
    rgClass --> rg2[Risk Group 2 - Moderate Pathogen - BSL 2]
    rgClass --> rg3[Risk Group 3 - Serious Indigenous or Exotic Pathogen - BSL 3]
    rgClass --> rg4[Risk Group 4 - Lethal No Treatment - BSL 4 Maximum Containment]
    rg2 --> ex2[Examples: Salmonella Staphylococcus Influenza HBV HIV]
    rg3 --> ex3[Examples: Mycobacterium tuberculosis Brucella abortus SARS-CoV-2]
    rg4 --> ex4[Examples: Ebola Marburg Lassa Smallpox]
    rg1 --> bsl1[BSL 1 - Open Bench - Lab Coat - Gloves]
    rg2 --> bsl2[BSL 2 - Class II BSC - Autoclave - Double Gloves]
    rg3 --> bsl3[BSL 3 - Negative Pressure - HEPA Exhaust - PAPR Suit]
    rg4 --> bsl4[BSL 4 - Positive Pressure Suit OR Class III Cabinet - Double HEPA]
```

---

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Controls — Occupational Biohazard Mitigation

```mermaid
graph TD
    level1[Level 1 - ELIMINATION - Remove the hazard entirely - Most Effective]
    level2[Level 2 - SUBSTITUTION - Replace with a less dangerous agent]
    level3[Level 3 - ENGINEERING CONTROLS - BSC HEPA filters Sealed centrifuges Ventilation]
    level4[Level 4 - ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS - SOPs Training Signage Restricted access]
    level5[Level 5 - PPE - Gloves Lab coat Respirator Face shield - Least Effective]
    level1 --> level2 --> level3 --> level4 --> level5
    note1[KTU Key Point - PPE is the LAST line of defense not the first]:::warn
    level5 --- note1
    classDef warn fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Biohazard Spill Response Operational Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    spill[Biohazard Spill Occurs] --> ev[STEP 1 - Evacuate 30 cm radius and stop work]
    ev --> settle[STEP 2 - Allow aerosols to settle 30 min for RG-2 60 min for RG-3]
    settle --> ppe[STEP 3 - Don PPE - Lab coat - Double gloves - N95 - Goggles]
    ppe --> contain[STEP 4 - Contain spill - Absorbent paper - Pour bleach from periphery inward]
    contain --> contact[STEP 5 - Allow disinfectant contact time 20 to 30 minutes]
    contact --> collect[STEP 6 - Collect into red biohazard bag - Autoclave]
    collect --> decon[STEP 7 - Final wipe down with disinfectant then water]
    decon --> report[STEP 8 - Report to Biosafety Officer - Document in incident log]
    report --> med[STEP 9 - Initiate medical surveillance if exposure suspected]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Biosafety Level 3 Facility Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    OUT[Outside - Public Area] --> CHG[Change Room - Clean Side]
    CHG --> ANT[Anteroom - Air Pressure Negative minus 30 Pa]
    ANT --> LAB[Main BSL-3 Laboratory - Negative minus 50 Pa]
    LAB --> BSC[Class II Biosafety Cabinet - HEPA Filtered]
    LAB --> AUTO[Double Door Autoclave - Decontaminates Exit Waste]
    AUTO --> OUT2[Clean Side Out]
    LAB --> EXH[Exhaust Air - Double HEPA Filtered]
    EXH --> ROOF[Roof Discharge - 3 m above roofline]
    PPE[Required PPE - Solid Front Suit - PAPR - Triple Gloves] -.-> LAB
    MON[Continuous Air Pressure Monitoring - Magnehelic Gauges] -.-> LAB
```

---

## 4.5 Biohazard Waste Segregation Flow (India BMW Rules 2016)

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Biomedical Waste Generated] --> YEL{Yellow Bag - Anatomical Soiled Chemical}
    START --> RED{Red Bag - Contaminated Plastic Tubing Bottles}
    START --> WHT{White Container - Sharps Needles Scalpels}
    START --> BLU{Blue Container - Glassware Broken Glass}
    YEL --> Y1[Incineration or Deep Burial]
    RED --> R1[Autoclave or Chemical Disinfection]
    WHT --> W1[Autoclave then Shred or Encapsulate]
    BLU --> B1[Disinfection then Glass Recycling]
    Y1 --> FINAL[Ash to TSDF Landfill]
    R1 --> RECYCLE[Sent for Plastic Recycling]
    W1 --> FINAL2[Disposed in Sharp Pit]
    B1 --> FINAL2
```
